title: Strategies of College Learning for Student Athletes
description: This is a course for first-year, first-season student-athletes. It focuses on stress and adjustment, eligibility, and special challenges for student-athletes.

title: Job Search Training
description: Provides training in all aspects of job search preparation including résumé writing and interview skills. Open to students in any major who are sophomore A.A. degree candidates or junior/senior B.A. or B.S. degree candidates. This course is repeatable.

title: The Oiler Experience
description: This course is an introduction to campus life and as such is designed for first-time, full-time freshmen. Topics will include transitional issues, study skills, fundamentals of time management, health, and wellness concerns. This course will not count toward the total hour requirement. This course will be graded S/U.

title: Strategies for College Success
description: Students will learn and be encouraged to use appropriate academic, social, and personal skills important for success in college. The course is open to all students; however, it is required of all second-semester freshmen who earn below a 2.0 GPA first semester, yet have taken ACAD 110. This is a developmental course. This is a repeatable course.

title: Exploring the Liberal Arts: Choices, Challenges, and Connections
description: This interdisciplinary seminar combines academic inquiry with the fundamental mission of the College of Arts, Humanities, and Social Sciences - to prepare students for lives of critical thinking, cultural awareness, and service. Course topics will vary each year, but all students will be exposed to issues in the humanities, arts, and social sciences. Classes will be small, will require substantial student participation, and will include an experiential learning component. Emphasis will be on developing and improving the reading, critical thinking, and communication skills necessary for success in college and beyond.

title: Portfolio Development
description: This is a course in which students learn the strategies for developing a portfolio. As part of this course, students will also write material for their individual portfolios. This course is repeatable.

title: Oiler Success Seminar II
description: This course is designed to meet the individual learning strengths, needs, and goals of students registered for this course. Students will learn effective and research-based strategies so their educational career goals are met through their degree program. This course can be taken twice for a combined total of two hours.

title: Oiler Success Seminar
description: This course is designed to support and enhance students' academic literacy through analyzing and implementing effective reading, learning, and study strategies. Students will learn how to access, interpret, and evaluate information and think critically about university-level course content.

title: Approaches to Career Development
description: Through the study of career planning theories and the factors that affect career choice, strategies for effective personal career decision-making and career development through the lifespan will be explored and applied. At various times the course may focus on special topics, such as career planning issues for adult learners. Repeatable up to four semester hours, provided the topics are different.

title: Intermediate Accounting II
description: This course is a continuation of ACCT 330 . It expands the underlying framework and concepts of financial accounting in the context of how accounting fits into the overall business environment of a contemporary society. The course provides a deeper understanding of the theory and practice related to financial accounting with an emphasis placed on special problems, which include leases, bonds, investments, shareholder's equity, earnings per share, accounting changes and errors, statement of cash flows, and financial statement analysis. International financial reporting standards are incorporated.

title: Financial Spreadsheet
description: Introduction to spreadsheets used to accumulate and analyze financial information. Special attention will be given to the financial functions used in business.

title: Accounting Club
description: In the club, students are given the opportunity to meet with accounting professionals. Additionally, students become exposed to professional trends, employment opportunities, and education growth within the accounting discipline. This course is repeatable. This course is graded S/U.

title: Principles of Managerial Accounting
description: This course is a continuance of ACCT 220 with emphasis on developing a basic understanding of financial accounting pertaining to business organizations by focusing on debt/equity financing, the statement of cash flows, time value of money, and financial statement analysis. In addition, the course introduces students to various managerial accounting topics including job-order costing, cost-volume-profit analysis, cost allocation, activity-based costing, internal controls, performance evaluation, capital budgeting, and master budgets.

title: Fundamentals of Accounting Information
description: This course is an introduction to financial and managerial accounting. The emphasis throughout the curriculum is on providing and utilizing accounting information to plan, control, and make effective decisions by applying principles of financial accounting, analysis of financial statements, and managerial accounting tools and techniques. Multiple functions of Microsoft Excel, such as financial modeling, reporting, and the automation of accounting tasks will be integrated to apply accounting skills to genuine business applications especially useful to businesses, not-for-profits, or other professions.

title: Intermediate Accounting I
description: This course is a continuation of the study of accounting principles. It expands the underlying conceptual framework and concepts of financial accounting in the context of how accounting meets the information needs of various users by developing and communicating information that is useful for external decision-making. The course provides a comprehensive review of the accounting process, a deeper understanding of the conceptual theory and practice of financial accounting, and a review of the preparation and understanding of classified financial statements in conformity with generally accepted accounting principles. International financial reporting standards are incorporated.

title: Taxation-Individual
description: This course provides an introduction to taxation in the United States. The primary orientation is the taxation of income. The rules for the taxation of income are found in Title 26 of the U.S. Code. The course will focus on research cases and problems oriented to answering questions relative to the application of the IRS Code. After an introduction to the basic tax model, the course examines gross income, its concept, and definitions of taxable and tax-exempt income. There follows a detailed examination of deductions and losses. In particular, self-employed business expenses, cost recovery, employee expenses, itemized deductions, and passive activity losses. Part of the assigned work is the completion of personal tax forms including Form 1040 and related forms. These forms are available from the IRS website, www.irs.gov. Shafer Library has access to legal databases, which permit the student to view code and regulation sections, as well as court cases relative to those code sections.

title: Principles of Financial Accounting
description: This course develops an understanding of the basic elements of financial accounting and the role of accounting in society. Students are introduced to fundamental accounting concepts, procedures, and terminology. Emphasis is placed on accrual accounting, revenue and expense recognition, accounting for assets, liabilities and equity, the accounting cycle, merchandise accounting, and internal controls. The course includes transaction analysis and the preparation and understanding of financial statements.

title: Accounting Information Systems
description: This course takes an applied approach to introduce students to the use of information technology in support of accounting events resulting from major business processes such as sales, purchasing, production, and human resources. Students will have hands-on experience using current computer based accounting applications. Students will be introduced to information systems concepts including database design and implementation, information security, and systems designs.

title: Accounting for Not-For-Profit Organizations
description: This course expands student's accounting knowledge by introducing students to the importance of government and not-for-profit accounting and the challenges that it presents. The course emphasizes the reporting requirements of the Governmental Accounting Standards Board (GASB) for state and local government, the Federal Accounting Standards Advisory Board (FASAB) for the federal government, and the Financial Accounting Standards Board (FASB) for the private sector, including not-for profits. The course develops an awareness of the issues surrounding government and not-for-profit accounting, the reasons behind them, their strengths and limitations, and possible alternatives.

title: Taxation-Business
description: The study of tax law and regulation relative to the formation of various business entities including the sole proprietorship, C-corporations, partnerships, and S-corporations is examined in this course. We continue the study of the tax code, tax regulations, and court cases, in this course related to the formation of a sole-proprietorship, corporation, or partnerships and distributions to its owners. Emphasis is placed on calculating taxable income and the resultant tax liability. Tax problems involve the completion of Form 1120 for C-corporations, Form 1120S for S-Corporations, and Form 1065 for partnerships. The nature of distributions to owners, shareholders or partnerships, is extensively discussed. Comparing and contrasting various business entities regarding formation, and tax consequences in each case is studied in depth in order to develop an understanding of what issues (both tax and non-tax) to consider when forming a business entity.

title: Cost Accounting
description: This course provides a study of the theories and practices of cost accounting with emphasis on job order, process, and standard costs.

title: Seminar in Managerial Accounting
description: This is a capstone course in the managerial accounting concentration. The class will examine situations where the accountant, examining the available financial information, must provide management with an approximate analysis and options for action. Students will have the opportunity to apply technical knowledge from prior accounting courses. Additionally, this course builds competency for students considering part one of the CMA exam.

title: Managerial Accounting
description: This course presents accounting as a management tool in the decision-making process, and covers budgeting, break-even analysis, and differential cost analysis.

title: Accounting Internship
description: This course provides qualified students with a one-semester opportunity to receive academic credit for supervised training and experience with a cooperating business firm supervised by the accounting faculty. It encourages students to utilize their acquired knowledge and skills in an actual work environment. Details are available from the program director/department chair, the business intern adviser, or the Office of Internships and Placements. This course will be graded S/U and may be repeated with a maximum of 12 semester hours applying toward a degree.

title: Seminar in Financial Accounting
description: This is a capstone course for the financial accounting concentration. It will explore two areas of vital importance to today's accountant including international accounting and professional ethics and corporate governance. The framework focuses on a manager's perspective, as regards the utilization and understanding of international accounting issues and the ethical implications of accounting decision making on various stakeholder groups.

title: Advanced Accounting
description: This course is a continued study of financial accounting theory and practice in selected areas of accounting. It includes an in-depth study of the accounting and reporting issues related to business combinations, consolidated financial statements, partnerships, and fiduciary accounting.

title: Auditing Theory and Practices
description: This course presents an introduction to auditing and other assurance services for students who have not had significant experience in providing such services. The primary emphasis is on the author's decision-making process, determining the nature and amount of evidence that must be accumulated after considering the unique circumstances of each engagement. Topic coverage includes professional ethics, legal liability, the elements of the audit process, and application of that process to various accounting cycles.

title: Alcohol and Other Drugs: The Fundamentals
description: This course will present an introductory overview of addiction. It will investigate historical and cultural attitudes towards various types of addictions; the physical, psychological, social, emotional, and spiritual factors surrounding addiction; and the clinical manifestations of both prevention and treatment. The concept of addiction as a disease will be examined.

title: Approaches to Addiction Treatment
description: This course will provide an emphasis on understanding modalities of treatment and care for diverse populations affected by substance abuse issues; including biology, psychology, and social aspects related to addiction. The course also explores a variety of helping strategies for reducing the negative effects of substance use, abuse, dependence, and addictive disorders.

title: Theory and Practice of Individual and Group Counseling
description: This course will provide a comprehensive overview of substance abuse screening and assessment with a particular focus on the 12-step recovery model. This course includes information related to interviewing techniques, substance abuse diagnosis, testing instruments, and issues or problems that influence the assessment environment. A variety of helping strategies for reducing the negative effects of substance use, abuse, dependence, and addictive disorders are covered in this course. Ethical issues related to the field of Addictions Counseling will also be explored.

title: First-Year Experience for Health Professionals
description: This is an introductory course for first-year freshman which will provide a sequence of enriching experiences offered by a team of health professionals that will enhance a student's knowledge of the various health professions. It will also introduce the student to the campus environment and provide information and training on the various resources that are available.

title: CPR and First Aid
description: The American Red Cross Community CPR and First Aid is designed to provide fundamental principles and knowledge of skills in adult, child, and infant CPR and standard first-aid practices. It prepares a person to meet most emergencies when medical assistance is not excessively delayed. This course is graded S/U.

title: Wellness: Strategies for Life
description: The class focuses on individual lifestyle choices and achieving balance in the following wellness dimensions: physical, intellectual, psychological, environmental, vocational, social, and spiritual. A variety of self-assessments and computerized inventories are utilized to assist the individual to develop a wellness profile, set personal goals, and develop a personal wellness plan.

title: Professional Ethics and Evaluation
description: This is a course that will discuss practical application of providing addiction services; assessment, placement, treatment planning, ethical/legal issues, and case management. The application for the Chemical Dependency Counselor Assistant license will be addressed as a possibility for students that has completed the minor/certificate requirements.

title: Foundations of Health, Physical Education, and Recreation
description: This course reviews the historical development of health and physical education, as well as sociological and psychological concerns. An introduction to the study of the role of play and its educational objectives is addressed. The evolution of athletics in our culture is also studied. Special emphasis is given to exploring these fields for potential careers.

title: Perceptual Motor Development and Learning
description: Physical, mental, and emotional factors underlying skill acquisition will be viewed to aid in preparing more knowledgeable teachers and coaches. Through various laboratory experiments, students will experience and understand the rationale behind many current teaching and coaching practices.

title: Water Safety Course for Training Instructors
description: This course is designed to prepare candidates to teach all levels of the Red Cross swim program up to and including safety training for swim coaches.

title: Human Nutrition
description: This course examines the nutritional needs of the human body for good health and to explore how nutritional needs change throughout the lifespan. Special attention is given to physiological function as students explore how diet impacts health both acutely as well as in the long term.  Considerations for food safety and global nutrition will also be discussed.

title: Community and School Health Education
description: This course addresses the principles, methods, materials, and resources involved in the teaching of health education. Students will be introduced to the organization and development of community and school (K-12) health education curricula. The course will focus primarily on positive prevention, enhancing health behaviors, and early intervention strategies.

title: Skills and Analysis of Individual Sports
description: Students will gain knowledge and basic sports skills related to individual sports. Students will also learn appropriate skill analysis techniques through the use of skills tests. Appropriate teaching methods for individual sports will also be discussed.

title: First Aid and Care of Athletic Injuries
description: The focus of this course is on evaluation of basic athletic injuries, proper preventative aspects, first aid, and immediate care procedures. It is open to elementary education majors and required for students majoring in health education.

title: Medical Terminology
description: This course covers formation of medical terms and their definitions.

title: Skills and Analysis of Dual Sports
description: Students will gain knowledge and basic sports skills in dual sports. Students will also learn appropriate skill analysis techniques through the use of skills tests. Appropriate teaching methods for dual sports will also be discussed.

title: Pathophysiology for the Health Professions
description: This course will explore how diseases affect the human body, including cellular, tissue, and organ system changes. It expands on knowledge of anatomical and physiological concepts to explain the etiology (cause), pathogenesis (development), and clinical manifestations (signs and symptoms) of various diseases. The course equips future health care professionals with a foundational understanding to inform patient care approaches.

title: Varsity Athletics
description: This course is intended for student athletes and must be certified by the head coach in which the student is participating. Students are eligible to repeat this course for a total of one semester hour.  This course is graded S/U.

title: Immersion in the Health Sciences
description: This course will be at on-site locations where students become familiar with real-world experiences. Students will develop an understanding of workplace culture, gain workplace competencies, initiate professional networking, and garner access to post-graduation employment opportunities. Students should develop personal time management skills and the enhancement of interpersonal relationships. Students will gain 80 hours of experience for this course.

title: Pathophysiology for the Health Professions Lab
description: This laboratory course provides applied learning experiences to reinforce pathophysiology concepts introduced in the companion lecture course. Students will examine disease-related changes at the cellular, tissue, and organ system levels and connect underlying mechanisms to clinical manifestations using case studies, models, and simulation-based activities.

title: International Health Care
description: This course explores global health issues and their effects on individuals and communities. It emphasizes the role of transportation and information technology in linking populations worldwide. Topics covered include disease transmission, health policy, and healthcare economics, with focus on how these factors influence international health systems. The course includes an international travel component designed to improve understanding of global health practices. Students should expect additional costs beyond tuition, roughly $2,500, to cover transportation and related expenses.

title: Adapted Physical Education
description: This course examines essential aspects of physical education for students with disabilities. Topics include safety, legal requirements for placement, and parental involvement. Students will learn to develop Individualized Education Programs (IEPs) based on assessments, abilities, and personal short- and long-term goals. Teaching methods such as task analysis and peer partnerships are explored. Practical experience is highlighted through fieldwork at Blanchard Valley Center and physical education classes within Hancock County Schools.

title: Principles, Organization, and Administration of Physical Education and Athletics
description: This course covers middle childhood through age 21. This course focuses on planning, teaching, and administering the school physical education curriculum, intramurals, and interscholastic athletic programs.

title: Human Sexuality
description: This class will be to prepare the health educator with methods and materials to teach human sexuality to students regardless of age, culture, or religious persuasion. Biological, psychological, and sociological aspects of human sexuality education will be addressed. This includes a study of the causes and prevention of sexually transmitted diseases and resources available. Through a variety of interactive activities and assignments, participants will learn the importance of human sexuality and how to teach and incorporate these ideas across the curriculum.

title: Physiology of Exercise
description: This course explores the science of exercise physiology including how the body responds to regulation and adjustment as affected by exercise. Influences of both acute and chronic exercise on the human body are examined and are applied to health, fitness, and exercise performance.

title: Educational Strategies in Health Professions
description: This course is designed to acquaint the student with the teaching/learning process including theories, skills, methods, and techniques. In this climate of change, it is necessary for the health care practitioner to be an effective educator of clients, families, colleagues, and the community. As a capstone project, students will organize an educational offering and present the materials to a targeted audience using instructional process and methods.

title: Philosophy and Practice of Coaching
description: This course examines the profession of coaching and involves studying the techniques and methods of coaching boys' and girls' interscholastic/intercollegiate athletic teams. Organization of athletic programs is studied with sociological implications considered.

title: Structural Anatomy
description: This course provides for the study of human surface and musculoskeletal anatomy. Anatomical structures needed for effective exercise prescription will be covered. Emphasis will be placed on muscular origins, insertions, and actions. Laboratory study (including cadaveric observation) will reinforce the principles covered during the lecture.

title: Practicum: Coaching Athletic Teams
description: Students will gain 80 hours of practical experience in observing, planning, and implementing athletic practice sessions and game strategies. These experiences will be completed in a variety of settings and sports under the supervision of experienced and successful coaches.

title: Sports Nutrition
description: This course covers the many aspects of nutrition as related to exercise and sport performance. These are the energy systems in exercise, nutritional aspects of substrate utilization (digestion, absorption, metabolism), assessment of nutritional needs, and diet modification for weight management, body composition changes, and/or sports performance.  Micronutrient needs, dietary supplements, and ergogenic aids, and nutrition-related conditions or disorders common to athletes will also be discussed.

title: Cardiovascular Rehabilitation Practicum
description: This course is designed for the student to put principles of cardiovascular rehabilitation, and foundations of cardiovascular physiology and electrocardiogram interpretation into practice in the clinical setting.  Students will work with an outpatient cardiovascular rehabilitation clinic to gain practical experience within the setting.  Students will also be required to work through required readings to better understand the continuum of care and the role of cardiovascular rehabilitation within that model.

title: Research in Health and Human Performance
description: The Research in Health and Human Performance coursework is intended primarily as an educational experience to acquaint students with the research process and provide a foundation for continued, independent professional growth. Students, in collaboration with a faculty member, will choose a topic of interest, critically interpret scientific literature, design appropriate research methods, instrumentation and procedures, collect, and analyze data to complete a research project, which contributes to the body of knowledge in health and human performance. This is a directed study under the supervision of faculty. This course may be repeated with a maximum of six semester hours applying toward a degree.

title: Applied Cardiovascular Electrophysiology
description: This course explores cardiovascular pathophysiology as it pertains to cardiovascular rehabilitation, and to educate students on electrocardiogram (ECG) interpretation so that they are competent in rhythm assessment while completing an ECG, watching a cardiac monitor, and/or conducting a stress test.

title: Introduction to Animal Science
description: This course is designed to introduce students to the various species of domestic livestock to develop a deeper appreciation of the animal agriculture industry and the animal sciences. Topics include animal anatomy and physiology, breeding and genetics, nutrition, general management practices, and other subjects of special interest.

title: Biomechanics
description: This course focuses on biomechanical fundamentals of human motion, joint structure as it relates to human movement, and functional movement patterns of the musculoskeletal system. Analysis of human movement and functional activities is also incorporated.

title: First-year Experience for Animal Science Majors
description: This course will assist students in making a successful transition to The University of Findlay both academically and personally. The course aims to foster a sense of belonging, promote engagement in the curricular and co-curricular life of the College of Sciences and the Department of Animal and Pre-veterinary Studies. This class will also articulate to students the expectations of the college and its faculty, help students develop and apply critical thinking skills, and help students continue to clarify their direction and goals in preparation for meaningful lives and productive careers.

title: Experiential Learning in Health and Human Performance
description: This course is intended for students to apply formal education to a "real world" experience. Through this course, students will have the opportunity to integrate classroom knowledge and theory with workplace practice while clarifying academic goals. Students will develop understanding of workplace culture, gain workplace competencies, benefit from professional networking, and enjoy increased access to post-graduation employment opportunities while strengthening personal time management skills and the enhancement of interpersonal relationships. All facilities must be approved by the instructor. The course may be repeated up to five semester hours applying toward a degree in wellness and exercise promotion.

title: Practical Animal Handling I: Equine
description: This course covers fundamental principles of handling, restraint, and veterinary medical techniques pertaining to horses. Coverage of equine breeds, aging of horses via dental exams, and other "hands-on" demonstrations will be among the topics covered. Students may not take this course for audit.

title: Practical Animal Handling II: Food Animals
description: This course covers fundamental principles of handling, restraint, and veterinary medical techniques pertaining to food animals with an emphasis on beef and dairy cattle, swine, sheep, and goats. Additional topics include neonatal care and management, live animal evaluation, and other related subjects. Students may not take this course for audit.

title: Research Methods in Health and Human Performance
description: Students will learn to apply statistics and research methods used in contemporary health and human performance settings. Emphasis is placed on understanding and applying common statistical measures, research design, and research dissemination.

title: Principles of Food Animal Nutrition
description: This course provides comprehensive coverage of the basic principles and applied aspects of general livestock feeding and nutrition. Topics include digestive anatomy and physiology, nutrient utilization and requirements of farm livestock, types and characteristics of feeds used in livestock rations, techniques in ration formulation, feeding programs, and recommendations for each of the major species of farm livestock.

title: Physiology of Reproduction and Growth
description: This course focuses on anatomy and physiology of reproduction in farm animals with emphasis on the unique differences in reproductive physiology among the major species of farm livestock. Topics may include reproductive endocrinology, artificial insemination, embryo transfer, genetics, breeding systems, care and management of breeding stock, among others.

title: Animal Products
description: This course provides a study of products derived from animal agriculture and marketed for human use. Topics covered include products produced, processing, marketing strategies, inspection and grading, health and nutrition, economics, food safety, and consumer concerns.

title: Principles of Meat Science
description: This course provides instruction to undergraduate students interested in attaining additional knowledge and experiences in the science, technology, and business of processing and marketing muscle foods.  Students will participate in discussions, demonstrations, and activities that introduce the principles of conversion of meat-type animals to human foods.

title: Principles of Animal Breeding and Genetics
description: This course covers a systems approach to selection and mating of livestock: gene frequency, heritability, relationship, inbreeding, linebreeding, heterosis, crossbreeding, direct and correlated response to selection, and the use of pedigree, family, progeny testing, and indices for selection.

title: Small Animal Medicine
description: This course is designed to enhance the small animal medical skills of pre-veterinary students. Topics of discussion will include physical examination of the dog and cat, collection and interpretation of laboratory data, diagnostic and therapeutic procedures, infectious diseases of the dog and cat, pharmacology, and preventative medicine.

title: Graduate/Professional School Seminar
description: This course is designed to prepare students for admission to a graduate and/or professional school program of study. The overall aim of the course is to serve as a source of current information on admissions trends and application procedures to assist students in preparing for admission to graduate/professional school.

title: Farm Business Management
description: This course provides comprehensive coverage of the basic principles of farm business management. Topics covered include budget management and decision making, measuring and managing performance of the farm, and acquiring resources. The emphasis of the course will be the organization and management of a farm in a manner that maximizes profit. This course includes the preparation of a farm business plan.

title: Forage and Crop Production
description: This course covers growth characteristics, adaptations, and utilizations of different plant species for hay, pasture, silage, and land conservation. This course covers the plant, animal, and environmental factors which relate to establishment, management, productivity, and utilization of forage plants.

title: Livestock and Agricultural Product Merchandising
description: This course provides instruction and hands-on experiences designed to provide students with the knowledge and capability of multiple techniques commonly used in the merchandising of livestock species and products related to the livestock industry. Students will participate in the demonstrations and activities that introduce various methods and techniques used in merchandising products of the livestock industry.

title: Food Animal Medicine
description: This course is designed to enhance the food animal skills of pre-veterinary students. Topics of discussion will include preventive medicine, diagnosis, treatment, and prevention of diseases in cattle, sheep, goats, and swine. Other in depth topics will include immunology, vaccination strategies, preventive medicine programs, and the economic impact of disease on animal agriculture.

title: Beef Production and Management/Lab
description: This course provides an in-depth study and practical application of the principles of beef production and management. Topics will reinforce the discussions covered in the lecture and could include breeding, nutrition, health, feedstuffs, housing and equipment, waste management, processing and products, and markets.

title: Beef Production and Management
description: This course provides comprehensive coverage of the fundamental principles and management systems involved in beef cattle. There will be an integration of principles of nutrition, breeding, physiology, and marketing into complete production and management programs. The structure of industry, enterprise establishment, systems of production, production practices, and improvement programs will also be addressed.

title: Dairy Production and Management
description: This course offers comprehensive coverage of the fundamental principles and management systems involved in dairy cattle and milk production. There will be an integration of principles of nutrition, breeding, physiology, and marketing into complete production and management programs. The structure of the dairy industry, enterprise establishment, production practices, and improvement programs will be addressed as well as performance standards, selection criteria, recordkeeping systems, product markets, reproduction options and production techniques for modern dairy operations.

title: Swine Production and Management/Lab
description: This course provides an in-depth study and practical application of the principles of swine production and management. Laboratories will reinforce the topics covered in the lecture and could include breeding, nutrition, health, feedstuffs, housing and equipment, waste management, processing and products, and markets.

title: Swine Production and Management
description: This course covers integration and practical applications of principles of animal breeding and genetics, health, nutrition, reproduction, housing and equipment, and economics of a swine operation. The structure and dynamics of the swine industry, and economic importance to the national economy, performance standards, selection criteria, product markets, reproduction options and production techniques for modern swine operations will be discussed.

title: Dairy Production and Management/Lab
description: This course provides an in-depth study and practical application of the principles of dairy production and management. Topics will reinforce the discussions covered in the lecture and could include breeding, nutrition, health, feedstuffs, housing and equipment, waste management, processing and products, and markets.

title: Sheep Production and Management/Lab
description: This course provides an in-depth study and practical application of the principles of sheep production and management. Topics will reinforce the discussions covered in the lecture and could include breeding, nutrition, health, feedstuffs, housing and equipment, waste management, processing and products, and markets.

title: Sheep Production and Management
description: This course covers practical application and integration of principles of nutrition, breeding and genetics, health, physiology, and marketing into a complete management programs. The structure of the sheep industry, pasture management and improvement programs as well as performance standards, selection criteria, product markets, reproduction options and production techniques for modern sheep operations will be discussed.

title: Horse Production and Management
description: This course involves practical application of the principles of nutrition, health, reproduction, breeding and genetics and marketing of horses with special considerations for managing private and commercial horse facilities and businesses.

title: Poultry Production and Management
description: This course covers integration and practical applications of principles of animal breeding and genetics, health, nutrition, reproduction, housing and equipment, and economics of a poultry operation, with an emphasis on chickens. The structure and dynamics of the industry, and economic importance to the national economy, performance standards, selection criteria, product markets, reproduction options, and production techniques for modern broiler and layer operations will be discussed.

title: Meat Animal Evaluation
description: This course involves live animal and carcass evaluation of beef, sheep, and swine, with discussion of tissue growth, tissue development, and general anatomy of the various animal species as it relates to food production. Also discussed will be factors that influence the product quality and marketability.

title: Principles of Food Safety
description: This course provides instruction to undergraduate students interested in attaining additional knowledge and experiences in the basic principles and practices of food safety.  Discussions, demonstrations, and activities that introduce students to biological and chemical hazards in food as a result of improper processing, packaging, handling, and storage will be covered.

title: Poultry Production and Management Lab
description: This course provides an inâdepth study and practical application of the principles of bird production and management with an emphasis on chickens. Laboratories will reinforce the topics covered in the lecture and could include breeding, nutrition, health, feedstuffs, housing and equipment, waste management, processing and products, and markets.

title: Horse Production and Management/Lab
description: This course provides an in-depth study and practical application of the principles of horse production and management. Topics will reinforce the discussions covered in the lecture and could include breeding, nutrition, health, feedstuffs, housing and equipment, waste management, processing and products, and markets.

title: Junior Seminar in Animal Science
description: This course will provide the student an opportunity to explore and identify career or graduate/professional school placement opportunities. Students will learn and implement skills necessary for entry into an animal science field or acceptance to graduate or professional school through interviewing, developing cover letters and resumes, and networking with potential employers.

title: Advanced Animal Nutrition
description: This course will expose students to the various current topics in food animal nutrition. It will continue to expand into nutrition and management of the various groups of companion animals that many producers and hobbyists are raising today. Topics covered will include diet formulation, examining nutritional influences on animal performance and reproduction, evaluation of nutrition and management facilities, and adjusting rations to meet different animal marketing needs.

title: Advanced Food Animal Reproduction Techniques
description: This course provides instruction and hands-on experiences designed to provide students with the knowledge and capability of multiple reproductive techniques commonly used in the livestock species. Students will participate in the demonstrations and activities that introduce the reproductive techniques used in each of the food animals.

title: Advanced Animal Reproduction
description: This course is designed to compare mechanisms of all major aspects of male and female reproductive physiology of food animals. Emphasis will be given to species variation in regard to reproductive function and to a detailed examination of key reproductive events in both genders.

title: Meat Products and Processing
description: This course provides instruction to undergraduate students interested in attaining additional knowledge and experiences in the principles of muscle food process technology, culinary technology, meat inspection and labeling, quality assurance and quality control procedures.  Students will be introduced to food technology processing, manufacturing, preservation, packaging, and merchandising of muscle foods through discussions, demonstrations, and activities.

title: Advanced Topics in Animal Science
description: This course involves study of selected topics in the animal sciences not included in regular offerings. This is a repeatable course.

title: International Animal Science Experience
description: This course is a practical, experience-oriented activity in which students will participate in an international experience associated with animal science or veterinary medicine. This course will be graded S/U and may be repeated with a maximum of 6 semester hours applying toward a degree and major requirements.

title: Internship
description: This course is a faculty-supervised practical, experience-oriented activity in which students will work on-site with producers, businesses, or agencies within animal science or closely related fields to gain professional experience not possible in a classroom setting. A minimum of 80 work hours are required for one credit. This course will be graded S/U and may be repeated with a maximum of 15 semester hours applying toward a degree. Only six hours can be granted toward an individual internship and only six hours can be applied toward the major requirements.

title: Research in Animal Science
description: This course allows for research in areas of interest in the animal sciences to individual students. Registration and selection of a project are subject to the approval of the instructor. This is a repeatable course up to six hours.

title: Introduction to Anthropology
description: A survey of major concepts and techniques used in anthropology. Students will exam and compare human organizations that define societies.

title: Senior Seminar in Animal Science
description: This course will connect a number of aspects of animal science education through speaking, writing, and synthesis of concepts to inform and educate peers about issues in the animal sciences. Topics addressed may include issues affecting animal agriculture, dealing with concerns of consumers and activists, involvement in public debate, the political process, and others.

title: 3D Design in Crafts
description: This course presents the fundamentals of three-dimensional arts through analysis of historical and contemporary works. The principles of design are explored by working with traditional craft media and techniques. Coursework encourages the development of creative problem solving skills. This course is open to non-art majors as an initial experience in art.

title: 2D Visual Fundamentals
description: This course presents the fundamentals of two-dimensional arts through analysis of historical and contemporary works. The principles of design are explored by working with a variety of two-dimensional media and techniques. Coursework encourages the development of creative problem solving skills. This course is open to non-art majors as an initial experience in art.

title: Selected Topics in Anthropology
description: This course is designed to meet current interests by examining selected topics in anthropology. Topics may focus on issues within one or more anthropological subdisciplines (archeological, biological, cultural, linguistics).

title: Introduction to Digital Design
description: This course is an introduction to the theory and practice of graphic design as an art and a means of visual communication. The emphasis will be on learning basic computer skills needed to create original digital designs and images for print and screen. Projects will provide practical experience in multiple professional design applications.

title: Anthropological Research
description: The student will be exposed to and participate in the scientific method through conducting literature review, hypothesis and research question development, research design, data collection, and data analysis. Students will present results orally and in writing to faculty and peers. This course is repeatable for up to 12 semester hours.

title: Art Appreciation
description: Art Appreciation is a lecture course introduction to the visual arts designed for the non-art major. The focus of this chronological survey course will be on important themes in art in addition to an introduction to different media and techniques.

title: Digital Photography
description: This course explores digital photography through aesthetic and sociological analysis of historical and contemporary works. Coursework focuses on creating, editing, and analyzing images and the development of creative problem solving skills. Students will supply their own digital camera. This course is open to non-art majors as an initial experience in art.

title: Introduction to Animal Ultrasound
description: This course provides a foundation in the basic principles of ultrasound imaging for the animal sciences. Students will gain the understanding and recognition of ultrasound equipment; receive instruction on the physics governing ultrasound imaging, and basic image orientation and scan planes. This course also provides students with experiential hands-on scanning in ultrasound for the animal sciences in a controlled laboratory environment.

title: Topics in Art
description: The course is designed to enable students and faculty to explore selected topics in art. This course will expose the student to the subject matter, issues, and thinking characteristics of the area. The content of the course will vary and may include timely issues and thematic developments in the arts, an integrative and comparative approach to studying the arts, or a historical analysis of the arts.

title: Introduction to Drawing
description: This course explores traditional drawing concepts and practices through analysis of historical and contemporary works. Coursework focuses on representational drawing and the development of perceptual awareness. Students learn various strategies for visual analysis. This course is open to non-art majors as an initial experience in art.

title: Drawing: Media and Concepts
description: This course explores the fundamentals of drawing through analysis of historical and contemporary works. Coursework focuses on experimentation with a variety of drawing media and processes and encourages the development of creative problem solving skills. Students learn various strategies for visual analysis and image development. This course is open to non-art majors as an initial experience in art.

title: Film Photography
description: This course explores film photography through aesthetic and sociological analysis of historical and contemporary works. Coursework focuses on film camera and darkroom techniques and processes and the development of creative problem solving skills. Film cameras are available for student use. This course is open to non-art majors as an initial experience in art.

title: Watercolor and Works on Paper
description: This course explores the nature of watercolor and works on paper through analysis of historical and contemporary works. Coursework focuses on experimentation with a variety of watercolor techniques and processes that utilize paper as the primary vehicle/support. Students learn various strategies for visual analysis and image development. This course is open to non-art majors as an initial experience in art.

title: History of Art I
description: This course covers the exploration of the visual arts from prehistoric times to the Gothic period through study of major trends in painting, sculpture, and architecture.

title: Printmaking
description: This course explores the process of printmaking through analysis of historical and contemporary works. Coursework focuses on traditional printmaking techniques and encourages the development of creative problem solving skills. Students learn various strategies for visual analysis and image development. This course is open to non-art majors as an initial experience in art.

title: Ceramics
description: This course explores the nature of clay through analysis of historical and contemporary works. Coursework focuses on traditional hand building and wheel throwing techniques and encourages the development of creative problem solving skills. This course is open to non-art majors as an initial experience in art.

title: Conceptual Illustration
description: This course will focus on creating commercial, editorial, and technical illustrations with a strong focus on problem solving for visual communication. Students will explore and use a variety of traditional and digital mediums.

title: Painting
description: This course presents the fundamentals of painting through analysis of historical and contemporary works. Coursework focuses on experimentation with a variety of painting processes and the development of perceptual awareness. Students learn various strategies for visual analysis. This course is open to non-art majors as an initial experience in art.

title: History of Art II
description: This course explores the developments in painting, sculpture, and architecture from the Renaissance to the present.

title: Figure Drawing
description: This is a studio class in which students draw from an unclothed model to improve traditional rendering skills with continued exploration in the use of graphite, charcoal, and ink. The emphasis is on visual realism, accuracy to the form, and understanding anatomy of the human body as expressed in drawings.

title: Typography
description: This course focuses on the creative use of typography and the technical skills needed to execute word-based designs for print and screen. The practical use of type for communication and aesthetics will be stressed. Students will study the historical developments of written languages, letterforms and typefaces, and apply knowledge in traditional calligraphy through the development of time-based digital media.

title: Graphic Design
description: This class will introduce graphic design history and fundamentals for printed media including integration of type and images. Students will produce work digitally, demonstrating conceptual and visual creativity, and technical skill. Presentations and critiques will be an integral part of the class.

title: Sculpture
description: This course presents the fundamentals of sculpture through analysis of historical and contemporary works. Coursework focuses on traditional sculpting media and techniques and encourages the development of creative problem solving skills. This course is open to non-art majors as an initial experience in art.

title: Advanced Photography
description: This is a continuation of ART 213 or ART 216 . Digital and film sections of this class are offered. Students will learn how to apply photographic techniques toward the fulfillment of aesthetic and conceptual concerns and will be encouraged to continue developing their unique "visual voice." Students will participate in opportunities to prepare and exhibit their work and will create a professional portfolio. Students are required to provide their own camera. This course is repeatable up to six semester hours.

title: History of Photography
description: This course will explore the history of photography from its beginnings to the present day. Students will study the technical development of photography, consider meaning in images within a social context, and critique photography as an art form.

title: Art Internship
description: This course requires work with a business, organization, or individual for the purpose of career-directed experiential learning in an art-related field. Admission to the course requires approval of the faculty advisor and application through the Center for Career and Professional Development. This course will be graded S/U and may be repeated with a maximum of 15 semester hours applying toward a degree.

title: Image Manipulation
description: Students will become proficient in retouching and manipulating bitmapped images using Photoshop and other digital applications. Focus will be on creating original designs and imagery for print and new media. An understanding of file optimization, resolution, color modes, and advanced technical skills will be developed.

title: Narrative Illustration
description: This course will encompass the processes of creating an illustrated picture book or graphic novel as a form of visual storytelling. Character development and storyboarding will be emphasized. Students will be encouraged to develop a personal illustration style in the medium of their choice. An overview of the publishing industry will be covered.

title: History of Visual Communication
description: This class will explore graphic design and illustration as an art form and method of communication throughout history. An analysis of semiotics, the social-political role of visual communication, and the impact of technology on aesthetics will be addressed.

title: Advanced Painting
description: This course involves emphasis on specialized painting problems and greater selectivity of media. The student is encouraged to explore in depth selected methods and techniques with the aim of approaching a unique personal vision and means of expression.

title: Travel Seminar in Art
description: The primary objective of this course is to introduce students to the history and contemporary culture of select countries through the experience of travel.

title: Advanced Ceramics
description: This course covers advanced ceramic processes. Students are encouraged to refine selected forms and techniques, working toward a personal aesthetic in this medium.

title: Advanced Graphic Design
description: Emphasis is on creative design solutions to solve visual communication problems for print, interactive media, and packaging. Students will be encouraged to develop a personal style with a strong understanding of graphic design history. Focus will be on implementation of professional practices and working as part of a team on real-world design projects.

title: Advanced Printmaking
description: This course offers greater selectivity of media. Emphasis is on various innovative printmaking approaches directed toward a more complete understanding of the techniques and concepts of the medium plus development of a personal aesthetic.

title: Research and Readings in Art
description: This course involves explorations into aesthetic and technical aspects of the visual arts in relationship to historical developments and contemporary trends.

title: Veterinary Spanish
description: This intermediate Spanish course will teach students Spanish vocabulary and grammatical concepts for use in a veterinary setting. Students will work with the communication skills of reading, writing, listening and speaking Spanish and gain an understanding of the cultural norms of Spanishâspeaking communities so that they may communicate effectively and appropriately with their clients.

title: Senior Portfolio and Exhibition
description: This course involves the preparation and presentation of a comprehensive exhibition of studio work as well as the production of a portfolio and rÃ©sumÃ©.

title: Professional Practices in Art
description: This course involves problems and projects directly related to a student's major or area of concentration. As part of this course, students will submit a written summary and deliver a public presentation of work completed during the semester. This course is repeatable up to six credit hours.

title: Topics in the History of Art
description: This course explores selected time periods, issues, and/or themes in art. Topics may include but are not limited to: American Art; Modern Art; Italian Renaissance Art; History of Photography; Art, Women, and Society. The course may be repeated, if topic is different.

title: Large Animal Husbandry and Medical Care
description: This course is designed for undergraduate students with an interest in preâvet, biology, or large animal management. Through a combination of classroom lectures, field exercises, labs, discussions, and practicums, students will be introduced to basic large animal medicine and care. This intensive course will help students understand the many factors that affect the needs, health, and wellbeing of large animals. Students will have the opportunity to work alongside doctoralâlevel educators, veterinarians, and animal caretakers. The course is repeatable with a maximum of 9 total credits provided the topics are unique.

title: Biomedical Engineering in Veterinary Medicine
description: This course is an introduction to biomechanics and the use of prostheses with an emphasis on animal examples. Students learn how to use engineering principles and design processes to solve health problems and restore normal anatomical function and quality of life to animals. Students will start by studying the foundations of bioengineering, biomechanics, and joint loading and then apply these principles to understand animal prosthetic case studies. The course is repeatable with a maximum of 9 total credits provided the topics are unique.

title: Exploring Marine Life through SCUBA Diving
description: This course provides direct access to observe and study the diversity of marine life up close for identification and study while SCUBA diving. As part of this course, students will become familiar with the basic theory and skills to safely dive in a small group under the direct supervision of a certified divemaster. The course is repeatable with a maximum of 9 total credits provided the topics are unique.

title: Advanced Sculpture
description: Advanced methods and materials are introduced. Students are encouraged to develop and explore a personal direction in sculptural expression.

title: Animal Positive Reinforcement Training
description: This course will cover how animal training is essential for improving captive animal welfare in zoos and rescues. In this course, students will learn from an experienced positive reinforcement animal trainer about the fundamentals of positive reinforcement target training. Students will learn many principles from how to train a new healthcare behavior to how to prevent and reduce aggressive and stereotypic behaviors. This course is beneficial for students wishing to pursue a career in veterinary medicine, animal behavior, animal training, or zoology. The course is repeatable with a maximum of 9 total credits provided the topics are unique.

title: Elephant Management and Medicine
description: This course focuses on elephant management and medicine including anatomy, physiology, behavior, husbandry, nutrition, diseases, and conditions as well as challenges often faced when managing elephants in a captive or wild setting. During in-person course sections, students will have the opportunity to help with husbandry and other volunteer tasks, study elephant behavior, and observe and participate in elephant medical treatments and/or routine veterinary care. The course is repeatable with a maximum of 9 total credits provided the topics are unique.

title: Veterinary Surgical Nursing
description: During this course, students work with veterinarians to provide spay/neuter and vaccination services to local companion animals. The setting allows students to gain handsâon practice and observation and will expose them to the skills and concepts of working as a veterinary technician or veterinary assistant in a surgical setting. The course is repeatable with a maximum of 9 total credits provided the topics are unique.

title: Companion Animal Veterinary Medicine
description: This course will prepare students to manage companion animals in a veterinary setting throughout their academic and professional career. Students will learn about safety in the veterinary workplace, proper handling, physical exams, eye and ear care, skin diseases, common diseases and conditions and their treatments, diagnostics and therapeutic techniques, spay/neuter techniques, and anesthetic monitoring. The course is repeatable with a maximum of 9 total credits provided the topics are unique.

title: Zoo Husbandry and Care
description: In this course, students will learn how to care for a variety of mammals, birds, and reptiles in a zoo setting including proper handling, husbandry, management, and enrichment. Students will also learn about zoo involvement in research and community education and assist in these projects, as well as learn from specialists in the conservation and care of specific species. Through a combination of lecture, lab, and vet and keeper shadowing, students will get the full experience of working at modern zoos. The course is repeatable with a maximum of 9 total credits provided the topics are unique.

title: Wildlife Conservation and Care
description: This course focuses on maintaining, caring for, and rehabilitating wild animals in a sanctuary, rescue, or rehab and release setting, with unique opportunities to learn about native mammals, reptiles, and/or birds. Students are taught by veterinarians and other professionals about the biology, physiology, husbandry, common conditions, and medical management of captive wild animals. Throughout this course, students will learn about specific threats to different species in the wild as well as measures being taken to promote conservation. The course is repeatable with a maximum of 9 total credits provided the topics are unique.

title: Introduction to the Biological Sciences
description: This course is an introduction to all living systems from unicellular organisms to the more complex, multi-cellular forms of life. Course content includes topics such as mitosis, meiosis, DNA structure and replication, animal systems, plant structure, and ecological based studies. These topics will be studied through a two-hour lecture component and a one-hour lab section each week.

title: Wildlife Research
description: This course focuses on studying a specific species, research collection, and data analysis. Students will assist in research projects including collecting data in the field and seeing first-hand the role the research plays in contributing to the conservation of the species. The course is repeatable with a maximum of 9 total credits provided the topics are unique.

title: Clinical Veterinary Medicine
description: This course will give students practical experience in an exotics veterinary clinic, wildlife center, and/or animal sanctuary/shelter. Students will observe husbandry, diagnostic, and treatment techniques. When appropriate, the supervising veterinarians may also ask for assistance depending on a student’s experience and abilities. Students will have the opportunity to see how husbandry, diagnostics, and treatment techniques are adapted to the unique management challenges at the center as well as the budget constraints of the animal owners or center. Students will encounter a variety of conditions and needs, which will help build examination, diagnostic, and research skills. Students are expected to observe and support veterinarians and assist only according to the veterinarian’s judgment of patient condition and the student’s experience level. The course is repeatable with a maximum of 9 total credits provided the topics are unique.

title: One Health Concepts in Action
description: During this course, students will learn about the application of medicine, veterinary medicine, and public health in conserving Earth. Through a combination of labs, lectures, and fieldwork, students will gain insight into the interconnectedness of human, animal, and environmental health. The course is repeatable with a maximum of 9 total credits provided the topics are unique.

title: First-Year Experience for Biology Majors
description: This course will assist students in making a successful transition to The University of Findlay both academically and personally. The course aims to foster a sense of belonging, promote engagement in the curricular and co-curricular life of the College of Sciences and the Department of Biology. This class will articulate to students the expectations of the college and its faculty, help students develop and apply critical thinking skills, and help students continue to clarify their direction and goals in preparation for meaningful lives and productive careers.

title: Tropical Biology, Conservation, and Ecology
description: This course is an introduction to the many animals and ecosystems of the tropics through classroom, field, and laboratory work. Students will learn the types of biomes and their history, threats, and conservation. Building on students’ understanding of ecology, they will learn about efforts by both the government and non-governmental organizations to protect threatened ecosystems through logging and/or fishing bans, national parks, and accelerated recovery techniques. Students will also learn about community perspectives on these sensitive ecosystems. Field work for this course will include SCUBA diving and/or snorkeling with a conservation component, depending on the program location. The course is repeatable with a maximum of 9 total credits provided the topics are unique.

title: An Integrated Approach to the Sciences
description: This course offers an overview of the major historical advances in the sciences as well as the frontiers of modern research. The course is designed to provide non-science majors with an integrated view of scientific investigations in the fields of physics, astronomy, chemistry, geology, and biology.

title: Oceanus
description: This is an integrated science course focusing on the marine environment to convey global science concepts. The course investigates physical, chemical, geological, and biological resources, ocean-atmosphere interactions, and current global environmental issues and is intended for non-science majors.

title: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology
description: This course offers an introduction to the structure and working of the human body with emphasis on how structure makes function possible and disruptions in either leads to disease. Special emphasis will be placed on the musculoskeletal, nervous, cardiovascular, and respiratory systems. The health risks associated with current behavioral and societal issues will be discussed, including drug and alcohol use, smoking, HIV, eating disorders, obesity, heart disease, etc.

title: Basic Microbiology/Lab
description: This course provides study in the classification, structure, and metabolism of microorganisms. Laboratory work emphasizes bacterial growth characteristics, methods of culture, and identification of organisms. The role of microbes in food microbiology, industrial, and clinical setting, with emphases on detection and control, are studied.

title: Anatomy and Physiology for Health Professions II
description: This course covers the fundamentals of human anatomical structure and physiological functioning.  Topics covered include the following systems: skeletal, joints, muscular, cardiovascular, immune, respiratory, and reproductive. Knowledge gained through this course is intended to enhance future clinical coursework and practice.

title: Basic Microbiology
description: This course introduces students to the fundamental principles in microbiology and their clinical relevance. Topics included in this course are microbial structure, metabolism, genetics, and introductions to immunology and epidemiology.

title: Introduction to Anatomy and Physiology/Lab
description: This course provides an introduction to the human body by examination of its structure and functioning. Exercises will reinforce the discussions of the corresponding body system being covered in lecture. Methodology used will include dissection, audiovisual material, and computer software.

title: Anatomy and Physiology for Health Professions I
description: This course covers the fundamentals of human anatomical structure and physiological functioning.  Topics covered include the following systems: integumentary, nervous, endocrine, digestive, and urinary. Knowledge gained through this course is intended to enhance future clinical coursework and practice.

title: Biology and Conservation of Big Cats
description: This course focuses on big cat management and/or conservation. The course will involve learning about the host organization’s work and gaining extensive experience with its animals while using the center’s site as a base from which to learn about big cats. Students will attend extensive classroom lectures and complete several labs in addition to participating in routine welfare checks, feeding, ethograms, enrichment planning, and implementation and observation of training sessions with big cats. The course is repeatable with a maximum of 9 total credits provided the topics are unique.

title: Biology I: Introduction to Cell and Molecular Biology Lab
description: Students conduct laboratory experiments in cellular and molecular biology that involve extensive use of both compound and dissecting microscopes, spectrophotometers, and equipment for isolating and manipulating and analyzing proteins and nucleic acids. These experiments emphasize hypothesis testing, experimental design, data collection and analysis, using simple statistical tests (e.g., chi square, regression, t-test), and both written and oral reporting of the results. This course is appropriate for students majoring in the sciences.

title: Anatomy and Physiology for Health Professions I Lab
description: This course will use a lab-based systems approach to clarify and enhance lecture topics.  Topics covered include the following systems: integumentary, nervous, endocrine, digestive, and urinary. Various technologies will be used to enhance future clinical coursework and practice.

title: Biology I: Introduction to Cell and Molecular Biology
description: A review of the properties of atoms, chemical bonding, water, polarity, and pH, and an introduction to the structure of major bio-molecules and their relationship to cellular structure and function. Topics also include the chromosomal theory of inheritance, mitosis and meiosis, the cell cycle, the biology of DNA and Mendelian/transmission, and molecular genetics.

title: The Art of Microscopy
description: This course is designed to introduce students to current technological practices and scientific advancements by exposing them to various forms of microscopy. Students will gain an understanding of the different types of microscopes, microscope optics, optical imaging, and preparation techniques for the various forms of microscopy. Students will develop and augment their skills in the use of microscopes as well as learn their history and advancements to better understand their applications. Although biological tissue preparation will be a major component, this course is intended to serve a broad range of students. Microscopic techniques and their application to various disciplines and career paths will be discussed.

title: Natural History and Conservation
description: Conservation is an interdisciplinary field focused on preservation of biological diversity and is approached with an intentional awareness of the natural world. This course provides an opportunity to observe and identify plants and animals in the field while also learning the threats to those species. Students will examine approaches used by biologists to balance human needs and protection of biological diversity.

title: Anatomy and Physiology for Health Professions II Lab
description: This course will use a lab-based systems approach to clarify and enhance lecture topics.  Topics covered include the following systems: skeletal, joints, muscular, cardiovascular, immune, respiratory, and reproductive. Various technologies will be used to enhance future clinical coursework and practice.

title: Biology II: Biodiversity, Form, and Function
description: This is an introductory course and the second course in the biological sciences for biology majors. It examines the variety of populations, species, and natural communities known as biological diversity. It surveys the five kingdoms (bacteria-like life forms, protozoan life forms, fungi life forms, plants, and animals) and three domains of life on the planet. The relationship of global systems as they pertain to the healthy function of the biosphere is explored.

title: Immunology
description: This course emphasizes the basic immune responses of the innate and adaptive systems. It applies the basic concepts of cellular biology and genetics to the biological basis of immunity, immunological reactions and human health.

title: Genetics
description: This course covers the study of the nature of genetic materials, the fundamental processes of gene replication, and the role of the gene in directing the formation of materials in the cell. Bacterial genetic processes will also be reviewed. Applications of genetic inheritance patterns and populations genetics will be studied.

title: Cell Biology
description: This course provides an in-depth study of structure, function, and molecular pathways of the plant and animal cells. Emphasis will be placed on modern molecular techniques, the functioning of membrane systems, cell-to-cell molecular interactions, cellular signal transduction, protein translation, apoptosis, cellular aspects of embryonic development, and the cellular mechanism of cancer, disease processes, and immune response.

title: Genetics Lab
description: Molecular techniques of genetic study and manipulation will be explored. Restriction mapping, PCR, electrophoresis, and bacterial transformation will be several of the topics discussed and utilized in the laboratory section.

title: Biology and Society
description: This course offers an examination of the societal impact and responsibilities of the scientific community. Emphasis will be on the changing public awareness of complex situations associated with rapidly emerging biological technologies. Legal, ethical, economic, political, and scientific issues will be analyzed through lecture and discussions.

title: Biology Seminar
description: The seminar course will expose biology majors to a wide range of current scientific issues presented. Topics will expand upon and apply knowledge obtained in core courses. Students will observe and familiarize themselves with proper communication of scientific ideas. This is a repeatable course. This course will be graded S/U.

title: Microbiology for the Pharmaceutical Sciences
description: This course offers an examination of microbiology from a human pathogen perspective and is tailored toward students within the pharmaceutical sciences. Emphasis will be placed upon the cellular biology of common human pathogens as well as the molecular basis of their associated diseases. Additional topics to be covered include antimicrobial agents, mechanisms of antimicrobial resistance, methods of clinical identification/diagnosis, and the human microbiome.

title: Human Anatomy & Physiology I
description: This course offers a study of the anatomical structure of the body as it relates to the functioning of the human body. The course begins at the cellular level and continues up to the entire organism, using the organ systems as the means of study. Various technological tools are used to encourage critical thinking in those topics that have societal impact on human health. Genetic influences on the body and its functioning are included. Special emphasis is placed on the integumentary, skeletal, and muscular systems.

title: Botany/Lab
description: This course provides a survey of major plant groups with regard to structure, function, reproduction, life histories, and uses. Emphasis will also be placed on field work including ecology, taxonomy, and identification of flowering and non-flowering plants.

title: Botany/Lecture
description: The fundamentals of plant biology, plant physiological and processes of metabolism will be studied. Students will learn to distinguish between reproduction processes in non-vascular, seedless vascular, and seed plants. Additionally, students will develop an understanding of evolutionary concepts through the study of plant diversity and contrast plant communities found in various biomes of the earth.

title: Human Anatomy & Physiology II
description: This course provides a study in the physiological functioning of the body as it relates to structure. Special emphasis is placed on the cardiovascular, respiratory, urinary, and endocrine systems as these systems are influenced by genetic mechanisms as to their functioning in the development of disease. Also included as topics of discussion are the societal issues affecting the body, such as smoking, drug usage, diet, and other factors of lifestyles seen today. Laboratory exercises further clarify lecture topics. Various technologies are used to enhance class discussions.

title: Human Anatomy & Physiology/Lab
