description: This survey course introduces students to key communication concepts such as media literacy, interpersonal communication, public speaking, and communication in organizations and groups. Students will demonstrate communication skills through oral, written, and mediated communication.

title: Media Literacy
description: New technology has revolutionized the traditional mass media model to make everyone a potential publisher. Students will examine the ways we acquire, create, and disseminate mediated information, study how traditional mass media outlets have evolved, and explore the influence of social media on society. Students will learn to evaluate media content and sources to polish information literacy skills.

title: Interpersonal Communication
description: This course offers an emphasis on dyadic communication with focus on listening, semantics, nonverbal communication, socialization, attraction, confrontation, and conflict resolution related to the intrapersonal variables of perception and self-concept.

title: Sport Communication
description: This course is designed to provide an analysis of various topics within the field of sport communication. This includes examines the sports communication industry including media relations, community relations, building a fan base, crisis communications, and careers in sport communication. Interpersonal communication in sports, organizational communication in sports, sport media, and sports criticism will also be discussed. Skills in writing, analytics, and strategy will provide a baseline understanding of communication theories and principles within sport communication.

title: Argumentation and Debate
description: This course provides a study of the principles of argumentation and the procedures of debate with special attention given to the collection of evidence, analysis, reasoning, organization, and practice in presentation.

title: Health Communication
description: This course offers an overview of ways in which perspectives of health have changed and influenced health practices and health communication. The course also examines communication strategies and media used to disseminate information and traces practices and policies that created the need to legislate privacy of health information.

title: Principles of Public Relations
description: This course provides a study of the history, development, and status of public relations in business, governmental, and ancillary organizations. Application of the duties of a practitioner and research methods as well as an introduction to planning, execution, and evaluative aspects of public relations are also covered.

title: Intercultural Communication
description: This course offers a survey of the variables that affect communication among peoples of different cultures as well as the use of this knowledge in facilitating such social and cultural interchanges.

title: Global Awareness Peer Program (GAPP)
description: The Global Awareness Peer Program exists to promote positive relations and interaction between university students from various backgrounds and cultures, help students develop in the areas of cultural humility and intercultural communication, and raise global awareness by focusing on the attitudes, knowledge, and skills necessary for students to competently and perceptively navigate and appreciate an increasingly inner-connected world. The course is repeatable and graded S/U.

title: Oral Interpretation
description: This course involves the oral reading of literature with special attention given to its selection, emotional meaning, and interpretation. Voice and diction will be emphasized as part of the course.

title: Audio Production
description: This course covers the theories and processes of producing, writing, and directing the audio in programs including special events, advertising, and promotion.

title: Media Writing and Reporting
description: This course teaches students how journalists and other media writers think, gather information, and write stories for media outlets on daily deadlines. Students practice journalistic skills, which include covering news events, conducting interviews, and writing and editing stories.

title: Newswriting Lab
description: Students will practice journalistic skills learned in COMM 251L by researching, interviewing sources, and writing basic news stories that are targeted at the University audience. Types of assignments will parallel topics being covered in COMM 251 throughout the semester. Students will work with the instructor to establish story angles, interview preparation, writing methods, and deadlines. Students will spend a minimum of 50 hours working as a reporter for The Pulse .

title: Political Communication
description: This course will study the functions, styles, strategies, and practices of political communication. Emphasis will be placed on students being media literate consumers and producers of political communication.

title: Social and Digital Media
description: This course is designed to introduce students to the primary areas of social and digital communication as they connect to the major areas of the media. Students will be introduced to and learn to work with social media strategies and analytics, photo and video shooting and editing software for these areas, recording and editing audio, and streaming applications for audio and video.

title: Non-Print Media Writing
description: Techniques and practical exercises in covering news stories and writing and editing for radio and other non-print mass media are included in this course. The course will utilize microcomputer word processing.

title: Nonverbal Communication and Listening
description: This course includes the study of nonverbal communication and the role it plays in human interaction, as well as the role listening plays in effective communication.

title: Media Theory and Research Methods
description: This course involves a study of the theories and research on the effects of media on the consumer and society.

title: Video Production I
description: This course introduces basic video equipment operation and aesthetic principles, begins the basics of studio operations and television lighting, and introduces students to producing and directing video projects. It presents practical training of pre-production, production, and evaluation techniques for broadcast and non-broadcast productions. Includes a separate lecture and lab to complete course work.

title: Communication Field Experience
description: Under supervision of department faculty, students will begin to use application of basic theory through practical experience in at least two programs of study within the communication area. It is expected that most students will be in the introductory-skill level and will gain experience in equipment use and application at this level. This course may be repeated for up to three hours. This course is graded S/U.

title: Communication Field Research
description: This course requires students to study qualitative and quantitative methodology and apply it to their field research and data analysis.

title: Junior Assessment
description: In this course students are required to create rÃ©sumÃ©s and evaluate the knowledge and skills they have developed thus far. Students will be introduced to Senior Assessment projects by becoming familiar with the proposal process, analyzing previous projects, and working with seniors in the process of completing their projects.

title: Advanced Public Speaking
description: This course provides extended study and application of the principles of public speaking with emphasis on content, organization, effective presentation style, and forms of proof. Students will prepare and deliver various kinds of speeches that are called on in a number of real-life situations.

title: Small Group Communication
description: This course offers a study of the principles and methods of small group communication with emphasis on both participant and leadership behavior and group dynamics.

title: Contemporary Theories of Communication
description: This course provides a broad survey of theories of human communication and their uses. Included are such areas as symbolic interactionism, signs, meaning, cognitive consistency, field theory, etc.

title: Relational Communication
description: This course focuses on interpersonal and relational communication theory and skills. Students will analyze, critique, and apply these skills as they pertain to a variety of professional and social situations.

title: Interviewing
description: This course examines both sides of the interviewing process. Students will learn strategies for asking questions and formulating appropriate responses in health interviews, survey interviews, recruiting interviews, employment interviews, performance interviews, and counseling interviews.

title: Current Issues in Communication I
description: This course will build on the knowledge gained from basic understanding of the major trade journals in communication with the focus being on exploration and explanation of the current issues and trends in the field. Greater emphasis will be placed on the students' understanding of the context of current issues and actions being taken in communication, and students will be expected to report in written and oral form the movement of these trends and the potential effects they have.

title: Public Relations Campaigns
description: This course provides analysis of the techniques used in the practice of public relations, advertising, and integrated marketing communications with special emphasis on the development of the news release, special events planning, press conference, and crisis management. This course includes investigation into the use of public relations, advertising, and integrated marketing communications in corporations, education, government, health care, media, science, and technology. Review and critique of selected case studies and application of techniques will be accentuated in assigned projects via campaign planning and implementation.

title: Media Law
description: This course examines the role of the federal government in regulating media, the protections of the First Amendment, and the evolution of social media laws initially designed for legacy media.

title: Communication in Conflict Resolution
description: This is a course in the study of the forms of conflict that exist in different communication settings and the communication strategies that can be used to alleviate conflict in a positive manner.

title: Film and Society
description: The aim of this course is to teach students a greater appreciation of the medium of film, to arm them with the knowledge to be informed consumers, producers, and/or critics of film, and to enable them to see the larger picture of how this artistic expression can convey meaning and context to fictional and non-fictional stories and events.

title: Critical Studies of Media and Communication
description: This research course pulls from the theoretical traditions of critical and rhetorical studies. It examines the intersection between media, communication, society, and identity.

title: Broadcast Newswriting
description: This course presents a theoretical and practical approach to news reporting, writing, gathering, and editing for the electronic media; the role and responsibilities of the newscaster and reporter; the impact of words and pictures as well as exploration of the legal and ethical issues of the broadcast news industry.

title: Feature Writing
description: This course begins with the basics and moves through increasingly advanced forms of feature writing for print and digital forms. Students will learn to generate feature story ideas, research the ideas through a variety of methods, conduct in-depth interviews, and write feature stories for a variety of audiences in multiple platforms. Students will also learn about the process of marketing stories for freelance writing.

title: Public Relations Writing
description: This course examines how public relations practitioners think, plan, and write within their profession. Students will learn to write news releases for multiple media outlets, develop and implement PR communication strategies for multiple audiences using traditional and social media platforms, research case study analyses, and produce other public relations-oriented materials.

title: Video Production II
description: This course is a continuation of COMM 260 , an intermediate study of video production techniques, including more complex procedures and special effects. Electronic field production operation and editing skills are emphasized. Increased emphasis will be placed on producing and video as an artistic expression and the effect these messages have on the audience.

title: Advanced Newspractice
description: This course builds on the basic skills taught in the introductory newswriting course. Students will learn to prepare professional journalistic articles, including opinion columns, in-depth features, spot news, and investigative articles.

title: News Editing
description: This course focuses on editing news and feature stories for accuracy, clarity, and completeness. Students will learn how to identify and correct weaknesses in journalistic writing. Students will use a professional news style in editing wire copy and locally produced copy. This course will also explore the managerial roles of editors.

title: Digital Storytelling
description: This course presents theoretical and technical approaches to digital storytelling, allowing students to hone the knowledge base and skills needed to tell professional digital stories across multimedia platforms. The goal of the course is to emphasize development and mastery of digital storytelling skills.

title: Communication Field Experience
description: With department faculty supervision, students will begin to increase their skill level on intermediate-level production, management, and research projects. Course focus is on practical experience in at least two programs of study within the communication area. It is expected that most students will be in the intermediate skill level and will gain experience in equipment use and application at this level. The course may be repeated for up to three hours. This course is graded S/U.

title: Senior Seminar
description: This course provides an in-depth analysis of a selected topic in communication. Topics vary depending upon the interest and expertise of the participating students and faculty. The course may be repeated, if the topic is different.

title: Digital and Professional Speaking
description: Students will expand upon the basics of public speaking to create and analyze presentations in the digital age. Skills in creating, presenting, and publishing digital presentations and videos for personal, professional, and entertainment audiences will be covered.

title: History of Communication Theory
description: This course offers a study of the major contributors to the theory, understanding, and practice of oral communication in the classical, medieval, Renaissance, and modern periods.

title: Communication Field Research
description: This course requires students to study qualitative and quantitative methodology and apply it to their field research and data analysis.

title: Senior Assessment
description: This course involves preparation and presentation of selected research projects relevant to educational training and professional goals. Students will produce a project in their selected medium and display/discuss/perform their work in a public forum.

title: Persuasion
description: This course involves a study of the nature and methods of persuasion as they relate to oral communication with emphasis on increasing the student's skill in persuasive speaking and ability to recognize and evaluate the persuasive appeals.

title: Organizational Communication
description: This course will include the study of communication theory as it is applied in organizational settings, the monitoring of organizational communication strategies and the determining of their effectiveness, the study of the various media used in organizational communication and the guidelines for making use of each, the analysis of the various audiences to which organizational communication is directed, and a polishing of the basic communication skills that students must have to communicate effectively in organizations.

title: Advanced Video Production
description: This course is an advanced-level examination and practical application of video production skill and theory. Greater emphasis on advanced applied media aesthetics, z-axis control, and pre- and post-production processes. Students will complete a series of projects increasing in difficulty and use of production elements to demonstrate visual competency and acuity.

title: Communication Arts Internship
description: This course involves a one-semester work experience with a business or organization that specializes in some area of communication. Admission to the course and credit for the course will be arranged in consultation with the instructor. This course should be taken during summer after junior year or during senior year. This course will be graded S/U and may be repeated with a maximum of 15 semester hours applying toward a degree.

title: Advanced Broadcast News Writing
description: This course is an advanced-level study of the practices, operation, and ethics of the broadcast newsroom. Students will study professional application and proceed through a series of assignments designed to raise their skill and decision making with greater emphasis on writing, interviewing, series-reporting, live coverage, and investigative reporting for the broadcast media.

title: New Media Design and Production
description: Students will be involved in the design and development of websites, apps, and other interactive media. Emphasis is placed on creating functional sites and apps with visual appeal. Understanding and addressing the needs of the user and workflow process will be stressed.

title: Developing Grant Proposals
description: This course offers an in-depth study of how to research, contact, and cultivate potential funders in the federal, state, corporate, and foundation sectors and how to prepare grant proposals, applications, cover letters, project descriptions, budgets, program reports, and other related documents.

title: Public Relations Student Society of America
description: The Public Relations Student Society of America (PRSSA) is a student-run public relations and corporate communications education program. Students of all majors will benefit from PRSSA in myriad ways, including: gaining leadership abilities, enhancing professional skills and résumés, learning about teamwork, and developing a large network for possible internship and job opportunities. This course emphasizes leadership and practitioner skills-building and is graded S/U. This course may be repeated up to three credit hours.

title: ACM Student Chapter
description: The University of Findlay Student Chapter of the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) is an organization of students who are prospective computer professionals and students who are interested in computers. Activities include regular meetings of the chapter, attendance at a meeting of the Northwest Ohio Chapter of ACM or a similar professional organization, tours of computer facilities, and sponsorship of invited speakers. This is a repeatable course. This course is graded S/U.

title: Advanced Multimedia Authoring
description: This course provides an examination and experimentation of multimedia tools and concepts. Students will be required to produce a multifunctional product integrating a variety of digital media, applying an appropriate interactive interface design, and incorporating concepts of usability.

title: Individual Study
description: For communication arts students, an independent study to secure an in-depth background in an area of communication that holds special interest to the student. This course may be repeated, if the topic is different.

title: First-Year Experience for Analytical Sciences
description: This course will assist students in making a successful transition to The University of Findlay both academically and personally. The course aims to foster a sense of belonging, and promote engagement in the curricular and co-curricular life of the College of Sciences and analytical disciplines. This class will also articulate to students the expectations of the college and its faculty, help students develop and apply problem-solving and critical thinking skills, and help students continue to clarify their direction and goals in preparation for meaningful lives and productive careers. This course is intended for students majoring in computer science, mathematics, or chemistry.

title: Presentation Graphics
description: This course will use presentation software for a comprehensive study of graphical tools and the techniques of creating effective and professional-looking presentations. Students will prepare for and take the Microsoft Office Specialist (MOS) PowerPoint Certification Exam as a part of this course.

title: Digital Literacy for Educators
description: This course is designed to prepare education majors to utilize technology as a tool in the classroom. Topics will include an overview of digital literacy topics pertaining to computers, devices, the Web, hardware for educators, software for educators, curriculum integration, and the evaluation of education technology and integration strategies. Laboratory work will include word processing, presentation graphics software, electronic spreadsheets, and database management using the Microsoft Office suite. Programming concepts will also be explored within the Alice programming environment.

title: Word Processing
description: This course provides a thorough study of beginning and intermediate word processing concepts including the design and development of professional documents and business publications. Intermediate topics will include multi-column and landscape printing, footnotes/endnotes, outlines, indexing, table of contents, sorting, mail-merge, graphics, tables, macros, and math/spreadsheet features. Students will prepare for and take the Microsoft Office Specialist (MOS) Word Certification Exam as a part of this course.

title: Digital Literacy for the Liberal Arts
description: This course is designed to provide an overview of digital literacy concepts pertaining to computers, devices, and the Web. Topics will include hardware, software, applications, and impact on society. Laboratory work will include word processing, presentation graphics, electronic spreadsheets, and database management using the Microsoft Office suite.

title: PC Operating Systems
description: This course will focus on the study of a commonly used microcomputer operating system such as Windows, Mac OS, or Linux. Students will learn how to customize the user environment; utilize new operating system features; manage folders, files, and software; optimize storage and system performance; enhance security; manage hardware and networks; and perform basic troubleshooting. Students will complete a major project as part of this course.

title: Spreadsheet Design
description: This course provides practical experience in the planning, design, and applications of an electronic spreadsheet package. Topics include general use of the spreadsheet, database, and graphics capabilities of the package, spreadsheet planning and design, and business and professional applications of spreadsheets. Students will prepare for and take the Microsoft Office Specialist (MOS) Excel Certification Exam as a part of this course.

title: Digital Content Management
description: This course is a study of beginning and intermediate Web site creation through the use of a content management system such as Microsoft SharePoint. A major project is a part of this course.

title: Database Applications
description: This course offers practical experience in the planning, design, and applications of a database system. Topics include file structure and creation, database queries, reports from a database, organizing database files, and combining database files. Students will prepare for and take the Microsoft Office Specialist (MOS) Access Certification Exam as a part of this course.

title: Introductory Java Programming
description: This is a beginning programming course with an emphasis on fundamental computer science concepts and the use of objects in Java. Topics include basic computer science concepts, limited use of the Java GUI environment, conditional statements, methods, looping, arrays, strings, and sequential input/output text files.

title: Microcomputer Topics
description: This course is a study of selected microcomputer hardware and software topics not included in the regularly offered courses. This course is repeatable, provided the topics are different.

title: Digital Literacy for the Sciences
description: This course is designed to provide a comprehensive overview of digital literacy concepts pertaining to computers, devices, and the Web. In addition, application development, algorithmic development, careers, and technology opportunities will be explored. Laboratory work will include word processing, presentation graphics, electronic spreadsheets, and database management using the Microsoft Office suite.

title: Introduction to Unix
description: This course is an introductory study of the Unix operating system with an emphasis on the practical aspects of the use of operating system utilities. Topics include processes, common commands and options, file and directory management and protection, text utilities, editing, and simple script usage.

title: Introductory Python Programming
description: This is a beginning programming course with an emphasis on fundamental computer science concepts using the Python programming language. Topics include basic computer science concepts, conditional statements, methods, looping, arrays, strings, input/output files, common libraries, and limited data analytics and processing.

title: Visual Basic Programming
description: This course introduces the concepts of structured programming and its use in problem solving, including problem definition, solution development, testing, and evaluation. Topics include the use of a visual programming environment, the use of various Visual Basic control objects, conditional statements, sub procedures and functions, looping, arrays, structures, text files, and database access. Good programming practices and interface design will also be introduced.

title: Computer Software Design I
description: This course introduces structured design using the C++ programming language, while continuing to enhance the understanding of structured programming practices and principles. Topics include the design and implementation of stream input/output, selection and repetition structures, modularity, arrays, searching, sorting, records, and string processing.

title: Digital Literacy for Business
description: This course is designed to prepare business majors to utilize technology as a tool in the business world. Topics will include an overview of digital literacy topics pertaining to computers, devices, the Web, hardware, software, applications, and impact on society. Laboratory work will include introductory and intermediate experiences with electronic spreadsheets and database management using the Microsoft Office suite. Students will be given the opportunity to earn Microsoft Office Specialist Certification in Excel and Access as a part of this course.

title: Web Page Development
description: This course offers a practical experience in the creation of beginning and intermediate Web pages using HTML coding. Students will learn how to write HTML code to format text, add color and images, and utilize tables, frames, and forms. Additional topics such as the use of JavaScript within their Web pages and creating a well-formed XHTML document will be covered as time permits.

title: Computer Software Design II
description: This course introduces object-oriented design and implementation using the C++ programming language, building upon the understanding of structured design and implementation gained from CSCI 240 . Topics include encapsulation, polymorphism, inheritance, overloading, pointers, and recursion.

title: SQL & Business Intelligence Tools for Decision Making
description: This course is a beginning course on writing basic and intermediate SQL code using a standard relational database package such as Access, SQL Server, or MySQL. In addition to writing queries, students will learn to use a business intelligence (BI) tool such as Tableau and/or Power BI to create sophisticated data visualizations which can help users quickly answer questions about trends, exceptions, and hidden insights within large data sets.

title: Discrete Structures
description: This course covers the study of the theory and applications of discrete mathematics for the computer scientist. Topics include analysis of algorithms, induction proofs, Boolean algebras, relations, digraphs, trees, finite state machines, languages, and grammars.

title: Topics in Computer Science and Technology
description: This is a flexible elective available as a regular offering or for independent study. Topics may include emerging technology, current technological hardware and/or software tools, and/or contemporary technological matters of interest. Registration and selection of topic are subject to approval of the instructor. This is a repeatable course, provided the topics are different.

title: Operating Systems
description: This course covers operating systems terminology, concepts, and design theory including memory management, processor, and process management, device and file management, concurrency, and system management. Common Windows-based and Unix-based operating systems will be used as case studies.

title: Information Security Fundamentals
description: This course offers an introduction to information security including security policy; anatomy of abuses (theft, fraud, viruses, worms, DoS); risk analysis; auditing and vulnerability assessment; firewalls and intrusion detection systems; and home and small business security.

title: C# Programming
description: This course introduces the C# programming language, applying and refining skills learned in previous programming courses. Topics include becoming familiar with setting up and using the C# programming environment, utilizing common C# tools, applying visual programming paradigms for desktop and mobile interfaces, and advancing skills in computer programming and software design.

title: Computer Organization
description: This course offers a study of the internal organization of computers. Topics covered include processor organization, data representation, information flow, instruction types, addressing, and structured machine levels.

title: Data Communications
description: This course provides a broad study of data communication, network organization, and network communication protocols. The course will provide the student with both theoretical understanding and practical experience in such things as signal processing, transmission media, local area network protocols, and wide area network protocols.

title: Data Structures
description: This course involves the study of data manipulation and data storage through object-oriented programming techniques. Topics include lists, stacks, queues, trees, sorting, searching, and memory management.

title: Python Programming
description: This course introduces the Python programming language, applying and refining skills learned in previous programming courses. Topics include becoming familiar with setting up and using the Python programming environment, and using selection structures, repetition structures, functions, recursion, strings, arrays, lists, tuples, dictionaries, sets, modules, classes and objects, common libraries, and data processing.

title: Internet Programming Unix
description: This course involves development of Web applications using Unix and a programming language such as PHP. Use of a database management system such as MySQL will be included.

title: Secure Ecommerce
description: This course is an examination of eCommerce and the security issues that arise therein. Topics included in this course are system administration, distributed systems security, operating systems security, database security, biometrics, and disaster preparedness. Major topics include National Security Telecommunications and Information Systems Security (NSTISS) planning and management, NSTISS policies and procedures, life cycle management, access control, administration, security policies and safeguards, along with risks and vulnerabilities associated with eBusiness initiatives.

title: 3C Computer Repairs Experience
description: This experiential learning course will give students the opportunity to learn multiple facets of working for a small computer and mobile device repair company which include hardware repair, marketing, and customer service. As a part of the course, students will gain knowledge and skills needed to obtain the A+ computer service certification. This course will be graded S/U and is repeatable up to a total of eight semester hours.

title: Managing Information Security
description: This course provides the foundation for the management of and strategy development for information security from the enterprise perspective. Topics include security planning and policy considerations, security program development, security management models and practices, risk management, security skills and certifications, and laws and ethics.

title: Professional Development
description: This course will cover everything from a warm greeting and handshake to resume building, job seeking, interviewing, confident and effective communication, socializing and networking, finding and utilizing a mentor, and lifelong learning. In addition, topics such as basic financial management and investments, employee benefits, and taxes will be explored as well as other important considerations as the student makes the transition from student to professional.

title: Using Cryptography
description: This course will focus on cryptography and its use in securing computer systems. Real-world applications of cryptographic primitives and protocols, and their limitations and vulnerabilities will be explored. Topics covered in this course will include ciphers and fundamentals, secret key encryption, hashing, public key, key exchange, authentication and digital certificates, tunneling, crypto cracking, light-weight cryptography and other methods, blockchain and crypto-currency, zero-knowledge proof (ZKP) and privacy preserving, wireless cryptography, and stream ciphers.

title: Computer and Network Forensics
description: This course explores the field of computer forensics with an emphasis on evidence collection, preservation of evidence, and data analysis. Topics will include the scope and impact of computer crime, forensics basics, forensic tools and their usage, access and control, incident handling and reporting, collection and preservation of evidence in a way that is acceptable in a court of law, legal and ethical considerations, and policy and statutes.

title: Data Mining Methods of Business Analytics
description: This course will focus on data mining techniques used to explore data, quantify and explain relationships between data and for prediction and classification of data. Topics covered in this course will include descriptive techniques of data exploration, data preprocessing and data reduction methods, predictive analytics, and text/social media analytics.

title: Computer Systems Administration
description: This course provides an advanced study of computer systems administration of either a Unix-based or a Windows Server-based operating system. The course will provide the student with both theoretical understanding and practical experience in such things as user management, process control, data backups, software installation, and system security.

title: Statistical Methods of Business Analytics
description: This course will focus on the study of statistics from a business analytics perspective to give practical experience on how statistics can be used to help professionals make better decisions based on data.

title: Information Assurance Capstone
description: This course will bring the previous learning together and help prepare students to become information assurance professionals. It will include a major research project and/or a community service component, grant writing principles, and participation in the management of a symposium. Topics will include development and implementation of security policies, internal security practices, roles of various personnel, system security validation, life cycle management, and grant writing. The students will learn professional standards, global management principles, and the principles of co-existence in a secure cyberspace.

title: Applied Operations Research
description: Topics will include linear programming, sensitivity analysis, network analysis, CPM, PERT, queuing calculations, and simulations. There is an emphasis on the use of software packages for problem solving.

title: JavaScript and Android Application Development
description: JavaScript and Android applications will be developed. JavaScript and jQuery JavaScript library functions will be used to create web applications. JavaScript will extend the functionality of the CSS and HTML content that is covered in other courses. The Android Studio IDE will be used to develop Android mobile applications using XML and Java.

title: Computer Networking
description: This course offers an advanced study of distributed computing environments and network architectures. The course will provide the student with both theoretical understanding and practical experience in such things as network design and implementation, network security, capacity planning, risk assessment, and feasibility studies.

title: Database Management Systems
description: This course covers the concepts of design and implementation of database management systems, focusing primarily on the relational model.

title: Software Engineering
description: This course is a study of software engineering including planning, requirements analysis, system design, program design, implementation, testing, delivery, and maintenance. Students will learn how to develop large software systems. Each student will participate as an active member of a team in the development of a software system.

title: Internet Programming Windows
description: This course is an introduction to Internet scripting from both the client- and server-side. Covers core Web programming concepts necessary for Web programming in JavaScript, VBScript, and ASP.

title: Undergraduate Research Project
description: The student, in conjunction with a department adviser, will select a semester-long project that utilizes the student's skills and integrates the student's learning in the field of computer science. This course will be graded S/U and is repeatable with no more than 12 semester hours counting towards the required 124 credit hours.

title: Internship in Computer Science
description: This course involves a guided work-study experience with a cooperating organization approved and supervised by the Department of Computer Science. Students will keep a detailed log of their work experience and will prepare a final report. Up to 12 hours may be earned in one semester. This course will be graded S/U and may be repeated with a maximum of 15 semester hours applying toward a degree.

title: Introduction to Sonography Lab
description: This course will use a lab-based approach to enhance lecture topics. Topics include screen orientation, patient position, image acquisition, and optimal machine settings.

title: Sonography Student Club
description: This club will provide opportunities for sonography students to learn more about the profession, participate in activities that build interpersonal skills, and determine educational and career goals. There is required participation in selected volunteer experiences. The course is graded S/U and is repeatable for up to five semester hours.

title: Introduction to Sonography
description: This course focuses on defining the scope of practice of the sonographer in the clinical setting and the basic skills required for patient care. Topics will include communication skills, basic vital signs, medical emergencies, invasive procedures and isolation techniques, history of sonography, career options, and professional societies.  Scanning planes are presented to include correlation with other imaging modalities.  Equipment knobology, sonographic characteristics, and scanning principles will be introduced.

title: Physics and Instrumentation
description: This course provides a comprehensive introduction to the principles of ultrasound physics and instrumentation. Topics include sound wave propagation, acoustic parameters, attenuation, reflection, refraction, Doppler effect, hemodynamics, and transducer technology. Students will explore the interaction of ultrasound with different tissue types, image formation, resolution, and artifacts. Additionally, the course covers the principles of bioeffects, safety, and quality assurance. Emphasis is placed on applying physical principles to optimize image quality and diagnostic accuracy.

title: Clinical Anatomy
description: Exploration of cross-sectional anatomy in the human body with an emphasis on ventral body cavities and organs of sonographic interest.  Pathogenesis of common disease processes and alteration of organ morphology and function will be discussed. Correlation of pertinent patient history, laboratory data, and imaging information will be emphasized to support development of analytical skills necessary for clinical practice. Emphasis will be placed on common pathologies encountered by the sonographer.

title: Language of Sonography
description: This course introduces the fundamental principles of medical terminology as applied to sonographic procedures and patient care. Students will develop proficiency in understanding and using medical terminology related to anatomy, pathology, and diagnostic imaging. The course also covers the basics of medical records, documentation, and healthcare communication, including electronic health records (EHR) and legal considerations. Emphasis is placed on correlating patient history, clinical indications, and laboratory values with sonographic examination criteria to enhance diagnostic accuracy. Through case studies and real-world scenarios, students will learn to apply critical thinking skills in integrating patient data with imaging protocols.

title: Capstone
description: This course will introduce the student to basic interview skills, professional portfolio and resume development. Emphasis will be placed on the Society of Diagnostic Medical Sonographers (SDMS) scope of practice and career options.

title: Echocardiography I
description: This course provides a foundation in the principles of echocardiography and gray scale duplex imaging of arterial and venous sonography. This course involves understanding and recognition of normal cardiovascular anatomy, the electrophysiology of the heart, the conduction system, and mechanical events of the cardiac cycle. This course discusses mechanical and electrical events in cardiovascular hemodynamics.

title: Echocardiography II
description: This course provides a foundation in the principles of pre-load and after-load, valvular disease, ischemic cardiac diseases, myocardial diseases as well as endocardial diseases, pulmonic arterial diseases, disease of the aorta and great vessels, and trauma. Each section of diseases will be discussed in detail regarding causes, signs, symptoms, echocardiographic findings, and complications.

title: Clinical Anatomy Lab
description: This course will use a lab-based approach to enhance lecture topics. Topics include anatomic appearance, relational anatomy, and organ identification.

title: Echocardiography II Lab
description: This course will use a lab-based approach to enhance lecture topics. Topics include sonographic appearance of various cardiac diseases, sonographic assessment for diagnosis, and additional protocol images required when encountering pathologies.

title: Echocardiography I Lab
description: This course will use a lab-based approach to enhance lecture topics. Topics include normal cardiac structures, sonographic views of cardiac imaging, heart wall segments, and routine diagnostic measurements.

title: Echocardiography IV
description: This course covers prosthetic valves, cardiac surgeries in adult population, pericardial diseases, and cardiac tumors. Each section of diseases will be discussed in detail regarding causes, signs, symptoms, echocardiographic findings, and complications. This course also discusses echocardiographic evaluation of cardiac surgery patients.

title: Echocardiography III
description: This course involves understanding of clinical pharmacology. Pharmacology, indications and contraindications of common drugs used in cardiac patients. Pharmacology of provocative stress agents and their uses/adverse effects will be discussed. This course also discusses potential side effects of cardiac medications on the cardiac function and the related echocardiographic findings.

title: Echocardiography IV Lab
description: This course will use a lab-based approach to enhance lecture topics. The main focus will be on complete performance of a diagnostic quality echocardiogram.

title: General Sonography I Lab
description: This course will use a lab-based approach to enhance lecture topics. Topics include sonographic appearance of abdominal organs, image acquisition of abdominal protocols, and routine diagnostic measurements.

title: Clinical Education II
description: This course will provide basic instruction and scanning experience in sonography in a hospital or other healthcare setting. The student will be able to demonstrate the ability to perform basic sonographic examinations according to the protocols established by the program and healthcare facility utilizing sonographic equipment at the level of an entry-level sonographer.

title: Echocardiography V
description: This course also involves understanding the indications and utility of advances in echocardiography such as; stress echocardiography, transesophageal echocardiography, intraoperative echocardiography, contrast echocardiography, and also 3D echocardiography and echo guided procedures.

title: Clinical Education I
description: This course will provide basic instruction and scanning experience in sonography in a hospital or other healthcare setting. The student will be able to demonstrate the ability to perform basic sonographic examinations according to the protocols established by the program and healthcare facility utilizing sonographic equipment.

title: General Sonography I
description: Discusses the normal anatomy and physiology related to the major abdominal organs, major abdominal vessels and superficial structures in the adult and pediatric patient. Topics include sonographic appearance, clinical assessment, evaluation protocols, technical factors, and image quality.

title: Advanced Topics in Computer Science and Technology
description: This course involves special studies and/or research in computer science and technology. Emerging technologies will be emphasized. Registration and selection of topic are subject to approval of instructor. This is a repeatable course, provided the topics are different.

title: General Sonography II
description: Discusses the normal anatomy and physiology related to the breast, non-gravid female and pediatric pelvis, and normal obstetrics. Topics include sonographic appearance, clinical assessment, evaluation protocols, technical factors, and image quality.

title: General Sonography II Lab
description: This course will use a lab-based approach to enhance lecture topics. Topics include sonographic appearance of pelvic and fetal anatomy, image acquisition of pelvic protocols, and routine diagnostic measurements.

title: General Sonography V
description: This course is designed to provide students with the sonographic examinations of the emergency department, emerging technology, interventional procedures, program and laboratory accreditation, and the Scope of Practice and Clinical Standards for the Diagnostic Medical Sonographer.

title: General Sonography IV Lab
description: This course will use a lab-based approach to enhance lecture topics. Topics include sonographic appearance of various pelvic and obstetric disease states, sonographic assessment for diagnosis, additional protocol images required when encountering pathologies, and performance of diagnostic quality sonographic examinations.

title: General Sonography III
description: Discusses pathology of the major abdominal organs and major abdominal vessels and superficial structure in the adult and pediatric patient. Topics include sonographic appearance, clinical assessment, evaluation protocols, technical factors, and image quality.

title: General Sonography IV
description: Discusses the pathology related to the non-gravid female and pediatric pelvis as well as obstetrical exams. Topics include sonographic appearance, clinical assessment, evaluation protocols, technical factors, and image quality.

title: General Sonography III Lab
description: This course will use a lab-based approach to enhance lecture topics. Topics include sonographic appearance of various disease states, sonographic assessment for diagnosis, additional protocol images required when encountering pathologies, and performance of diagnostic quality sonographic examinations.

title: Clinical Education I
description: This course will provide basic instruction and scanning experience in sonography in a hospital or other healthcare setting. The student will be able to demonstrate the ability to perform basic sonographic examinations according to the protocols established by the program and healthcare facility utilizing sonographic equipment.

title: Vascular Sonography II Lab
description: This course will use a lab-based approach to enhance lecture topics. Topics include sonographic appearance of pathology within the peripheral vascular system, image acquisition of protocols, diagnostic measurements, sonographic report writing, and abnormal finding evaluations.

title: Vascular Sonography II
description: This course is designed to provide students with pathology of the peripheral vascular system in adult and pediatric patients. Topics include sonographic appearance, clinical assessment, evaluation protocols, technical factors, and image quality.

title: Principles of Macroeconomics
description: This course offers an introduction to macroeconomic theory including national income accounting and the theory of income, output, and employment. Emphasis on application of national fiscal and monetary policies in a business context is discussed from a liberal arts philosophical point of view.

title: Vascular Sonography I Lab
description: This course will use a lab-based approach to enhance lecture topics. Topics include sonographic appearance of the peripheral vascular system, image acquisition of protocols, and routine diagnostic measurements.

title: Introduction to Economic Thinking
description: This course is an introduction to basic social science and economic reasoning techniques and the application of these skills to the study of several current economic problems.

title: Vascular Sonography I
description: This course is designed to provide students with the normal anatomy and physiology related to the major peripheral vessels, in the adult and pediatric patient. Topics include sonographic appearance, clinical assessment, evaluation protocols, technical factors, and image quality.

title: Principles of Microeconomics
description: This course provides an introduction to microeconomic theory including the economics of the firm, resource allocation, and international economics. Emphasis is on the study of pricing by supply/demand analyses in a free enterprise economy and with respect to international trade problems. Lectures are presented from a liberal arts philosophical point of view.

title: Labor Economics
description: This course builds on the principles learned in ECON 200 and ECON 201 to examine labor economics, trade unionism, collective bargaining, wage determination, employment and labor legislation as they apply in industrial relations and business contexts from both a contemporary and historical perspective.

title: Intermediate Microeconomic Theory
description: This course builds on the principles learned in ECON 201 to expand the understanding of microeconomic theory in our economy and society. It has an emphasis on consumer theory, producer theory, market structure, market and pricing strategy as these relate to efficiency and competition in a contemporary dynamic world economy.

title: Intermediate Macroeconomic Theory
description: This course builds on principles learned in ECON 200 to expand understanding of macroeconomic theory in our economy and society. It focuses on measurement and structure of national economies, economic performance, business cycle theory, fiscal and monetary policy implications on growth, unemployment, and price stability.

title: The Oiler Experience for Education Majors
description: This course is an introduction to the College of Education and campus life for all first-time, full-time education majors. Topics will include navigating the College of Education, the University, personal growth, leadership, and current issues in education. This course does not count toward the total hour requirement. This course will be graded S/U.

title: Principles of Econometrics
description: This course will cover the basic methods of data analysis used in economics, including descriptive statistics, hypothesis testing, and regression analysis. Various methods of regression optimization as they relate to economics will also be discussed.

title: Introduction to Special Education
description: This course is an introduction to the philosophical, historical, and legal foundations of special education in public education. The course will explore the characteristics and abilities of students with disabilities, the Federal Laws surrounding their education including the Individuals with Disabilities Education Act (IDEA) and students' right to a Free Appropriate Public Education in the Least Restrictive Environment, optimally within the general education classroom. Emphasis is placed on including all learners in an integrated setting as well as the collaborative roles of the general education teacher and intervention specialist regarding referral and teaming.

title: Managerial Economics
description: This course builds on the principles learned in ECON 200 and ECON 201 to show how economic theory and decision science tools can assist in the formulation of optimal solutions to managerial decision makers. The course incorporates contemporary management issues and analyses of current business behavior.

title: Clinical Education II
description: This course will provide basic instruction and scanning experience in sonography in a hospital or other healthcare setting. The student will be able to demonstrate the ability to perform basic sonographic examinations according to the protocols established by the program and healthcare facility utilizing sonographic equipment at the level of an entry-level sonographer.

title: Teachers of Tomorrow
description: This activity allows education students to expand and apply topics discussed in the regular university classroom. This student organization provides professional development opportunities and social support for future teachers. This course is repeatable and graded S/U.

title: Cultural Perspectives
description: This course delves into the rich tapestry of human cultures, equipping students with the critical tools to navigate an increasingly interconnected world. The course will explore diverse cultural expressions, belief systems, and social structures, fostering a deep understanding of what shapes individual and collective identities. Students will identify their own cultural perspective and make meaningful comparisons to other cultural perspectives.

title: Introduction to Teaching
description: This course introduces students to a variety of topics related to the teaching profession, such as teacher licensure, Ohio Learning Standards, lesson planning, teacher evaluations, professional ethics, and dispositions, as well as a clinical experience in a school setting. The Level 1 portfolio is attached to this course.

title: Clinical Practice I
description: This course will prepare students for their first field experience. In class, students will engage in coursework surrounding the Ohio Standards for the Teaching Profession, Professionalism, Licensure Code of Conduct, Differentiation and review of lesson planning. Students will further prepare for program key assessments. In addition, students will then attend their field experience that meets in an inclusive or departmental class/discipline setting, under the supervision of a Cooperating Teacher and a University Supervisor. Placement assignments will align with the age/grade band of the license the preservice teacher is pursuing.

title: Evaluating Human Impact on the Environment through Childrenâs and Early Adolescent Literature
description: This course will explore and analyze current environmental issues and human impact on the environment via literature created for children and early adolescent students.  Concepts associated with environmental issues will be addressed and examined through the use of picture books and chapter books.  Students will research proactive, practical steps to reduce their footprint which will be demonstrated through project-based learning objectives using science, technology, engineering, art, and mathematics (STEAM) methodology.

title: Video Games & Learning
description: This research-intensive course engages students in thinking deeply about games and interactive media and strengthens students’ critical thinking about research and media reports on games. Course topics include examining learning principles embedded into games; exploring models of situated, participatory, and interest-driven learning; and investigating games in a variety of content areas, including STEM, history, and literacy. Students will engage with current topics in the games and learning community by critically analyzing sources and composing arguments backed with evidence through a variety of reading, writing, and research assignments, including a culminating research paper.

title: Orientation Seminar for Preservice Teachers
description: This seminar is designed for candidates at the undergraduate and post-baccalaureate levels who have transferred into the program and who need to fulfill the requirements of Level I Assessments. Candidates will become familiar with the components of the University of Findlay’s College of Education Portfolio System and learn more about the history of special education and 13 disability categories under IDEA. Candidates will learn how to use the COE lesson plan template, Canvas on-line course delivery system, and TaskStream.

title: Theories of Growth and Development
description: This course examines current research on child and adolescent development and its application in the classroom. The promotion of healthy development and learning in educational and community contexts is a focus.

title: Clinical Practice II
description: This course will prepare students for their second field experience. In class, students will engage in coursework surrounding unit and lesson planning, classroom management, and using data to inform instruction. They will further prepare for more advanced program key assessments. The students will create a portfolio that addresses teaching expectations. In addition, students will then attend their field experience that meets in an inclusive or departmental class/discipline setting, under the supervision of a cooperating teacher and a university supervisor. Placement assignments will align with the age/grade band of the license the preservice teacher is pursuing.

title: Education and Disability Rights in Italy
description: This course examines the integration of students with disabilities in the Italian education system through the lens of civil and human rights. Students will engage with historical and contemporary perspectives on disability rights, policies, laws, and practices in Italian education as a means of comparison to the United States’ historical context of special education. The course combines classroom instruction with field visits to schools, policy institutions, and advocacy organizations in Italy. This course will be graded S/U.

title: Assessment/Evaluation for Middle Childhood, Adolescent/Young Adult, and Multi-Age
description: This course focuses on developing teacher candidates’ understanding of and skills in designing valid and reliable classroom assessments. Candidates will design and implement their own assessments as part of the junior-level field experience. Candidates will also learn how to interpret student assessment data and design next steps in teaching based on data.

title: Phonics and Foundations of Literacy
description: This course examines the developmental process of learning to read. Neural, perceptual, cognitive, and linguistic processes involved with reading will be discussed as these relate to how humans move from printed text to comprehension. The roles of phonics and phonological awareness play in literacy development will be a focus. Other essential elements of literacy including vocabulary, fluency, and comprehension will be addressed. Current research will be applied to designing and practicing evidence-based assessment, instruction, and intervention. This course covers all relevant state and federal mandates.

title: Technology Integration: Instructional Strategies
description: This course is designed to provide teacher education candidates with opportunities to explore, plan, and create a wide range of technology enhanced standards-based learning experiences for P-12 students. A major focus of this course is to support the teacher candidates’ abilities to research innovative classroom technologies, to engage in collaborative projects, and to produce useful and meaningful technology-supported lessons for P-12 classrooms.

title: Structured Literacy
description: This course will include the fundamental principles and elements of structured literacy instruction. The complexities of language as well as how to teach it in a systematic, explicit, and cumulative manner to support positive literacy outcomes will be a focus. Formal and informal assessment and instruction in phonology, morphology, syllabication, semantics, and syntax will be addressed. Current research will be applied to designing and practicing evidence-based assessment, instruction, and intervention. This course covers all relevant state and federal mandates.

title: The School Volunteer Program
description: This program is designed to provide volunteer opportunities for pre-service teachers to gain additional experience with the teaching/learning process in various school settings. Candidates enrolling in this course will volunteer a specified number of in-classroom clock hours based on the recommendations of the director of teacher education policy and field experience and the candidate's program director. This is a repeatable course.

title: Assessment and Intervention
description: This course develops an understanding of issues and specific skills related to assessment, diagnosis, and evaluation of students. Developmentally appropriate formal and informal assessment methods will be discussed with opportunities for field-based experience that allow students to apply their knowledge of child development by interpretation of observational data. Interpretation of assessment results and the development of Individualized Education Plans (IEPs) and other instructional and behavioral interventions are included.

title: Reading Intervention
description: This course combines developmentally appropriate literacy assessment and instruction to design effective interventions for students who are not meeting target benchmarks for the various aspects of the reading process. Selecting high-quality instructional materials for these purposes will be explored. This course covers all relevant state and federal mandates.

title: Teaching All Learners Together
description: This course will consider multiple facets of effective teaching, including the creation of a supportive course climate, the design and implementation of accessible classroom practices and assessments, and the selection and use of varied course content. The course will offer recommendations for accommodations, modifications, differentiation, adaptive and assistive technology, strategy development, and related services. Included will be ways to adjust methods, materials, environment, and curricula, as well as techniques to develop appropriate goals and objectives for students with disabilities within the general education classroom.

title: Classroom Management for Middle Childhood, Adolescent/Young Adult and Multi-Age
description: Candidates study the philosophy and structure of classroom environments and construct management strategies that impact the following: curriculum, instruction, classroom climate, student behavior, and attitude management. Candidates incorporate these skills into their field experience, while developing skills such as working collaboratively with school personnel, managing and preventing inappropriate student behavior, and designing learning environments for all students. This course is part of the junior methods field experience and should be scheduled with EDUC 302 , EDUC 303 , EDUC 415 , and appropriate methods classes for various licenses.

title: Child Development
description: Coursework includes an investigation of child development and its impact on learning. The work of cognitive and educational theorists will be introduced and explored to determine appropriate expectations for children in each stage of development. Information about individual differences among children and the support systems in place for teachers will be highlighted.

title: Early Intervention
description: This course provides a cross-disciplinary examination of the major tenets guiding practice in the field of primary education from historical and philosophical perspectives. Various models of delivery systems are examined to guide the student in the formulation of a philosophy of primary education. The course emphasizes knowledge and skills necessary for effective and resourceful primary education teachers of all children, including the special requirements of educating preschool children and the environments of teaching in a Pre-K through grade 5 classroom.

title: Integrated Science Methods and Curriculum for Middle Childhood and Adolescent/Young Adult
description: This course focuses on the curriculum, pedagogy, instructional materials, and technology used to effectively teach science in accordance with the Ohio Learning Standards for both middle and high school students in diverse contexts. All assignments will be completed in the grade bands of the candidate's license. This course is part of the methods field experience (junior level) and should be scheduled with EDUC 302 , EDUC 303 , EDUC 354 , EDUC 415 , and appropriate methods classes for various licenses.

title: Integrated Math Methods and Curriculum for Middle Childhood and Adolescent/Young Adult
description: This course focuses on the curriculum, pedagogy, instructional materials, and technology used to effectively teach mathematics in accordance with the Ohio Learning Standards for both middle school students and high school students in diverse contexts. All assignments will be completed in the grade bands of the candidate's license. This course is part of the methods field experience (junior level) and should be scheduled with EDUC 302 , EDUC 303 , EDUC 354 , EDUC 415 , and appropriate methods classes for various licenses.

title: Health and Physical Education Method
description: This course is part of the methods field experience (junior level) and should be scheduled with EDUC 302 , EDUC 303 , EDUC 354 , and EDUC 415 . Methods and materials that can be utilized in a school health physical education program, K-12, will be examined, practiced, and assessed. Consideration will be given to the planning, organization, and administration of age and developmentally appropriate health and physical education. Curriculum scope and sequence will include the evolution of elementary and secondary physical education programs, introductory activities, fitness development, fundamental skills and developmental stages, educational movement, rhythmic activities, games of low organization, tumbling and gymnastics, and lead-up games to team sports. Health and wellness topics will focus on holistic components of physical, mental, and social health and be embedded within the physical education units.

title: Integrated Social Studies Methods and Curriculum for Middle Childhood and Adolescent/Young Adult
description: This course focuses on the curriculum, pedagogy, instructional materials, and technology used to effectively teach social studies in accordance with the Ohio Learning Standards for both middle and high school students in diverse contexts. All assignments will be completed in the grade bands of the candidate's license. This course is part of the methods field experience (junior level) and should be scheduled with EDUC 302 , EDUC 303 , EDUC 354 , EDUC 415 , and appropriate methods classes for various licenses.

title: Integrated Language Arts Methods and Curriculum for Middle Childhood and Adolescent/Young Adult
description: This course focuses on the curriculum, pedagogy, instructional materials, and technology used to effectively teach English-language arts in accordance with the Ohio Learning Standards for both middle childhood (MC) and adolescent young adults (AYA ) in diverse contexts. All assignments will be completed in the grade bands of the candidate's license. This course is part of the methods field experience (junior level) and should be scheduled with EDUC 302 , EDUC 303 , EDUC 354 , EDUC 415 , and appropriate methods classes for various licenses.

title: Intervention Methods
description: This course will address methods, materials, strategies, resources, technology, assessments, and adaptations for students with disabilities. Topics include the effects of disabilities on content learning and development; evaluation of age appropriate instructional objectives; development of IEP objectives, and strategies for teaching skills in all content areas including language arts, mathematics, social studies, and science in the general education classroom or least restrictive environment with students receiving special educational services.

title: Modern Language Methods and Curriculum for Multi-Age
description: This course focuses on the curriculum, pedagogy, instructional materials, and technology used to effectively teach modern languages for Multi-Age (MA) licensure in diverse contexts. Coursework includes curriculum and pedagogy inclusive of all grade levels within the licensure band in accordance with the Ohio Learning Standards for World Languages and Cultures. This course is part of the methods field experience (junior level) and should be scheduled with EDUC 302 , EDUC 303 , EDUC 354 , EDUC 415 , and appropriate methods classes for various licenses.

title: Integrated Science and Mathematics Methods
description: This course covers the integration of problem-solving and skills approaches needed for success in science and mathematics. Students learn hands-on, inquiry-based approaches through activities that emphasize manipulatives. Class content aligns with the Ohio Learning Standards for mathematics and science.

title: Social Studies Content
description: Students will engage in content that aligns with the Ohio Learning Standards for Social Studies and the National Council for Social Studies (NCSS) content standards. Throughout the semester, students will examine the state and national content standards, identify the progression of learning across developmental stages and grade levels, and begin to employ practical strategies for teaching them.

title: Collaboration and Consultation
description: This course will engage in appropriate consultation, collaboration, and cooperative practices for pre-service teachers working with students with disabilities. Specific emphasis will be given to the educator’s alternating role as consultant, collaborator, and cooperative partner with various stakeholders, to include parents, guardians, general educators, and other educational professionals, administrators, and community service personnel. Collaborative and cooperative practices related to special education, literacy services, and other educational support areas will be examined. The main purpose of this course is for students to examine the critical role collaboration has in education to improve educational opportunities for all students.

title: Science Content
description: Students will engage in content that aligns with the Ohio Learning Standards for science as well as the Next Generation Science Standards. Students will examine the state and national content standards, identify the progression of learning across developmental stages and grade levels, and begin to employ practical strategies for teaching them.

title: Mathematics Content
description: Students learn math content that aligns with the Ohio Learning Standards for Math as well as the National Council of Teachers of Mathematics (NCTM) standards. Throughout the semester, students will examine the state and national content standards, identify the progression of learning across developmental stages and grade levels, and begin to employ practical strategies for teaching them.

title: Postsecondary Transition Planning
description: This course will address important concepts and techniques for students with disabilities who formally enter into the secondary transition planning process of their Individualized Education Program. The course will examine a truly successful transition process to include comprehensive planning that is driven by the students with disabilities’ preferences, interests, needs, and strengths. Topics include concepts about transition and adult life planning, service and support for those assisting youth and adults with disabilities, enhancing self-determination and self-efficacy skills as well as sources of services, networks, and organizations for opportunities for full participation in community life.

title: Language Arts and Fine Arts Content
description: Students will engage in content that aligns with the Ohio Learning Standards for English Language Arts and Fine Arts as well as the National Council of Teachers of English (NCTE) standards for English Language Arts. Students will examine the state and national content standards, identify the progression of learning across developmental stages and grade levels, and begin to employ practical strategies for teaching them.

title: Integrating Reading Assessments and Instruction
description: This course in the reading core focuses on the central question, “How does assessment inform instruction?” The course focuses on a wide variety of reading assessments and programs. Students also tutor small groups of children who struggle with reading in the UF Clubhouse Reading Program. Topics include interpreting standardized and informal test results, identifying authentic assessments that provide multiple indicators of student progress, analyzing assessment tools, and creating appropriate instruction to enhance reading strengths and needs. This course covers all relevant state and federal mandates.

title: Capstone Seminar
description: This culminating course evaluates all relevant state and federal mandates including licensure-area education policy issues, legal rights and responsibilities, code of ethics, professional development, mentorship, performance standards, measures of school and teacher impacts on student performance, professional reflection, dispositions of successful teachers, and other relevant professional issues.

title: Reading and Writing Across the Content Areas
description: This course will focus on the developmental continuums of reading and writing necessary to access, comprehend, and communicate about texts across subject areas. Selecting and utilizing high-quality instructional materials and learning supports including assistive technology will be addressed. This course is scheduled as part of the methods field experience (Level III).

title: Seminar/Independent Study
description: Guided research or study in a particular area selected by the student and the major professor.

title: Classroom Management
description: This course explores strategies to facilitate positive and productive behavior in students with mild/moderate disabilities. Topics include effective management and design of the teaching and learning environment, development of social skills, laws and procedural safeguards related to behavioral management, ethical considerations of behavior management, strategies for crisis intervention/prevention, establishing student rapport, development of skills in problem solving and conflict resolution, and development of student self-esteem and self-control.

title: Level 1 Interpersonal Speaking and Listening
description: This English course is designed for non-native English speakers with speaking and listening proficiency of 1.0-4.5 on an IELTs or equivalent test. The course will introduce learners to basic interpersonal communication skills.

title: Student Teaching
description: Student teaching is the culminating professional field placement in which a candidate participates in all aspects of the teachers’ day. Students apply their knowledge of data analysis, instructional planning, classroom management, assessment and human development. This is a sixteen-week placement in the candidate’s licensure area. The student must follow the calendar of the cooperating teacher. A cooperating teacher and a university supervisor will support the teacher candidate. This course will be graded S/U.

title: Visual Art Methods and Curriculum for Multi-Age
description: This course focuses on the curriculum, pedagogy, instructional materials, and technology used to effectively teach Art for Multi-Age (MA) licensure in diverse contexts. Coursework includes curriculum and pedagogy inclusive of all grade levels within the licensure band in accordance with the Ohio Learning Standards for Visual Arts. This course is part of the methods field experience (junior level) and should be scheduled with EDUC 302 , EDUC 303 , EDUC 354 , EDUC 415 , and appropriate methods classes for various licenses.
