Likewise, it is equally challenging to master the numerous classes of infective organisms seen in contemporary pharmacy practice. This course serves as a bridge between an introductory microbiology course and high-level pharmacotherapy typical in a PharmD curriculum. The course is intended to enhance the preparation of PharmD students for subsequent study in infectious disease with emphasis on medical microbiology, anti-infective drug classes and mechanisms as well as risks associated with use of various classes of anti-infectives.

title: Leadership in Pharmacy
description: This course will develop pharmacy students’ leadership skills and they will learn how to apply them in the professional pharmacy setting. The course will offer an introduction to the principles and practices of leadership, including core competencies and characteristics of effective leaders. This course will equip students with the knowledge and skills necessary to become successful leaders in the profession of pharmacy.

title: Special Topics in Pharmacy
description: This course will provide the pharmacy student with the opportunity to expand his or her knowledge base from the required curriculum by exploring special interest areas within the profession of pharmacy. The course may include, but is not limited to, exploration of clinical controversies, unique areas of pharmacy practice, pharmaceutical industry topics, the impact of pharmaceuticals on the environment, and marketing of pharmacy services. The course is repeatable, if the topic is different.

title: The Pharmaceutical Industry-Current Perspectives
description: This is a highly interactive course designed to build a foundation and knowledge base about the pharmaceutical industry and to review issues that are pertinent to the pharmaceutical industry today. It is the intent of this course that the student will also gain insights into how the pharmaceutical industry is structured, how it operates, what issues the industry faces now and into the future, and what opportunities exist for career development for pharmacists within the pharmaceutical industry.

title: Advanced Community Pharmacy
description: This elective is designed to help students interested in community practice to develop and refine skills that will be necessary to enter this type of practice. The areas of focus are management, patient care services, traditional dispensing processes, over-the-counter products, communications, legal, staffing, interviewing, and problem-solving. The format of the class is a combination of traditional lecture/discussion and team-based learning.

title: Pharmacology, Pathophysiology, and Therapeutics of Infectious Diseases
description: This course prepares the student to assess and manage the drug therapy of diseases of infectious origin. The course is taught in an interactive style and integrates pharmacology, medicinal chemistry, pharmacokinetics, pathophysiology, and therapeutics. Over-the-counter and herbal therapies will be included where appropriate.

title: Pharmacology, Pathophysiology, and Therapeutics of Cardiovascular Disease
description: This course prepares the student to assess and manage the drug therapy of diseases of the cardiovascular system. The course is taught in an interactive style and integrates pharmacology, medicinal chemistry, pharmacokinetics, pathophysiology, and therapeutics. Over-the-counter and herbal therapies are specifically included.

title: Pharmacology, Pathophysiology, and Therapeutics of Respiratory Disease
description: This course prepares the student to assess and manage the drug therapy of diseases of the respiratory system. The course is taught in an interactive style and integrates pharmacology, medicinal chemistry, pharmacokinetics, pathophysiology, and therapeutics. Over-the-counter and herbal therapies are specifically included.

title: Pharmacology, Pathophysiology, and Therapeutics of Immunology and Connective Tissue Disease
description: This course prepares the student to assess and manage the drug therapy of diseases of the immune system and connective tissue. The course is taught in an interactive style and integrates pharmacology, medicinal chemistry, pharmacokinetics, pathophysiology, and therapeutics. Over-the-counter and herbal therapies are specifically included.

title: Pharmacology, Pathophysiology, and Therapeutics of Endocrinology and Metabolism
description: This course prepares the student to assess and manage the drug therapy of diseases of the endocrine and metabolic systems. The course is taught in an interactive style and integrates pharmacology, medicinal chemistry, pharmacokinetics, pathophysiology, and therapeutics. Over-the-counter and herbal therapies are specifically included.

title: Introduction to Pharmacy Practice Experience III
description: Students involved in experiential education are engaged in structured, practice-related activities where knowledge gained from didactic education is coupled with a professional attitude and skills training resulting in the development of an entry-level pharmacist. PHAR 491 and PHAR 492 are designed to provide students with introductory experiences in community and institutional settings. Students will be expected to observe and participate in the dispensing aspects of pharmacy. Students will be required to obtain 100 hours of pharmacy experience during their second professional year (50 hours during PHAR 491 and 50 hours during PHAR 492 ).

title: Comprehensive Diabetes Management for the Pharmacist
description: This course provides an advanced understanding of the principles of diabetes management, moving beyond basic pharmacotherapy of this disease state to include management information from the disciplines of nursing, medicine, behavioral psychology, medical nutrition therapy, and exercise physiology.

title: Pharmacology, Pathophysiology, and Therapeutics of Renal Disease
description: This course prepares the student to assess and manage the drug therapy of diseases of the renal system and to optimize drug therapy in patients with pre-existing renal dysfunction. The course is taught in an interactive style and integrates pharmacology, medicinal chemistry, pharmacokinetics, pathophysiology, and therapeutics. Over-the-counter and herbal therapies are specifically included.

title: Pharmacy Practice Skills Lab III
description: This laboratory course will reinforce the Pharmacists’ Patient Care Process approach by preparing students to apply clinical assessment and therapeutic management skills in the treatment of one or more disease states, including the demonstration of medication administration and therapeutic monitoring techniques, assessment and management of comorbid conditions, effective sourcing, application and communication of drug information, and the development of the necessary soft skills for effective patient counseling and education. The course may also include case-based sessions, as well as the introduction of new material as appropriate to the learning session.

title: Introduction to Logic
description: This course provides an introduction to logic as the study of methods used to determine whether an argument’s premise(s) support(s) its conclusion. Topics may include symbolic logic, syllogistic or Aristotelian logic, Venn diagrams, truth tables, validity, rules of inference, propositional logic, and conceptual analysis.

title: Pharmacy Practice Skills IV
description: This laboratory course will reinforce the Pharmacists’ Patient Care Process approach by preparing students to apply clinical assessment and therapeutic management skills in the treatment of one or more disease states, including the demonstration of medication administration and therapeutic monitoring techniques, assessment and management of comorbid conditions, effective sourcing, application and communication of drug information, and the development of the necessary soft skills for effective patient counseling and education. The course may also include case-based sessions, as well as the introduction of new material as appropriate to the learning session.

title: Introduction to Philosophy
description: This course offers a survey of important philosophical problems relevant to human life in an age of rapid social and intellectual change, including the ultimate nature of reality, belief in God, personal identity, freedom and responsibility, the search for meaning in life, theories of knowledge, and ethical considerations of right and wrong.

title: Introduction to Pharmacy Practice Experience IV
description: Students involved in experiential education are engaged in structured, practice-related activities where knowledge gained from didactic education is coupled with a professional attitude and skills training resulting in the development of an entry-level pharmacist. PHAR 491 and PHAR 492 are designed to provide students with introductory experiences in community and institutional settings. Students will be expected to observe and participate in the dispensing aspects of pharmacy. Students will be required to obtain 100 hours of pharmacy experience during their second professional year (50 hours during PHAR 491 and 50 hours during PHAR 492 ).

title: Health Care Ethics
description: This course is designed to familiarize students with the kinds of ethical situations and questions they can expect to encounter as health care professionals, and to provide students with skills and information to enable them to make competent ethical decisions in clinical situations. Topics will include practitioner-patient relationships, treatment and informed consent, confidentiality, end-of-life choices, intraprofessional and interprofessional conflicts, and business concerns and conflicts. Intended primarily for students pursuing careers in health care and related fields, the course is open to all who are interested in the subject.

title: Ethics and Technology
description: This course is designed to familiarize students with the kinds of ethical situations and questions they can expect to encounter as professionals in the field of technology management, and to provide students skills and information to enable them to make competent ethical decisions in real-life situations. Topics will include information stewardship and privacy concerns, intellectual property issues, computer security, liability and safety responsibilities, professional codes of ethics, the social impact of computer technology, and related business concerns and conflicts.

title: Philosophy of Religion
description: This course offers critical reflection on the nature and origin of religion, the relationship between faith and reason, and the character of religious language. Attention will be given to questions concerning the existence of God, good and evil, life after death, and claims of religious knowledge.

title: Beauty and Value
description: This course is an inquiry into the nature of art and beauty, including problems of objectivity versus subjectivity. What does it mean to call something a "work of art?" Is beauty simply in the eye of the beholder?  It provides an application of philosophical ideas and theories to present-day experiences and ongoing debates in contemporary aesthetics, challenging students to articulate and defend these ideas in the context of specific issues and debates. This course is intended for students interested in the arts and creativity, but is open to all who are interested in the subject.

title: Business Ethics
description: This course provides a critical study of fundamental ethical issues in a business context. Topics include application of moral theory to real-life situations in business practice, policy, and relationships with a focus upon principles for making moral decisions.

title: Environmental/Animal Ethics
description: This course provides a historic and contemporary overview of Western and non-Western ethical thought concerning the environment and the humane treatment of animals. Students will be encouraged to critically examine their own beliefs, attitudes, and habits relative to the subject matter. Possible topics include saving endangered species, preserving the wilderness, controlling pollution, managing limited natural resources, raising animals for food, and using animals in experiments, as well as issues involving the moral status of animals, obligations to future generations, and global justice.

title: Sports Ethics
description: This course is designed to create awareness of important ethical issues in sports and to provide students with skills and information to enable them to competently respond to such issues as they occur. Building upon their own experiences with sports, students will explore real-life issues from a variety of perspectives (e.g., as coach, player, parent, umpire, citizen). Possible topics will include the role of ethics in athletic competition, the value of sportsmanship, fairness in sports, use of performance-enhancing drugs, gamesmanship, violence, the role of a coach as an authority figure, friend, and counselor, and whether sports build moral character. Intended especially for student-athletes, the course is open to all who are interested in the topic.

title: Logic and Language
description: This course acquaints students with the principles and methods of formal logic and its application to scientific reasoning, legal reasoning, and creative problem solving. It may also include an introduction to important issues in 20th-century philosophy of language.

title: Ethics and Education
description: This course provides an introduction to various age-old and emergent ethical issues in the world of education. Students will gain a familiarity with the role expectations of an educator and better appreciate the ethics of this vocation.

title: Media Ethics
description: This course offers a critical study of ethical issues in the field of communication and media, including print and non-print journalism, radio and television broadcasting, advertising, and to a lesser extent entertainment media such as movies and television programming. Possible topics will include the role of media in a free society, social and ethical responsibilities of media professionals, professional code of ethics, conflicts of interest, truthfulness and deception, privacy versus the public's right to know, when does "news judgment" become censorship, journalism ethics in the online age, what's fair in advertising and public relations, ethics and interviewing, and ethical dimensions of entertainment.

title: Appearance and Reality
description: This course is an inquiry into the fundamental nature of reality apart from what it merely appears to be. Students will consider classical and contemporary problems of metaphysics and their implications for everyday life. Topics include reality and illusion, mind/body dualism, change and becoming, the nature of time, God, and the very meaning of Being.

title: Social and Political Thought
description: This course provides a critical survey of salient social and political ideas in ancient, medieval, and contemporary philosophy. Although Western thought is given prominence, perspectives from non-Western cultures may be introduced to offer insightful comparisons or contrasts. Topics include issues of liberty, economic justice, law, authority, citizenship, and civil disobedience.

title: Existentialism
description: This course involves a survey of existentialist philosophy, including study of its influence and expression in literature, the fine arts, psychology, and religion. The course includes reflection upon basic existential themes such as the meaning of human existence, freedom and responsibility, the limits of reason, the significance of death, the individual versus society, and the implications of truth as subjectivity. Students will be challenged to consider how they define the world and to what extent they have the power to change the world. Readings from Kierkegaard, Nietzsche, Camus, Sartre, Buber, Simone de Beauvoir, or others are used within this course.

title: Experiential Learning in Philosophy
description: This course is designed to have students experience, reflect upon, and write about philosophical issues as they occur outside of the classroom. Field work may include off-campus interactions with other students in philosophy, leading the philosophy discussion group, coordinating campus-wide philosophy events, working with local schools, study abroad, working with community organizations, attending a professional philosophy conference, or preparing a paper for presentation at a conference and/or for publication. Students will be encouraged to consider concepts and themes that directly align with their planned vocation.

title: Topics in Philosophy
description: This is an advanced course that, in different years, may focus on special problems or particular philosophers. The course may be taken twice, provided the topics are different.

title: Knowledge and Understanding
description: This course explores the general question of what can or cannot be known. A distinction is made between knowledge claims, beliefs, and opinion, and the standard philosophical definition of knowledge as "justified true belief" is also presented and explored. Topics examined may also include classical and contemporary ideas and theories of knowledge, truth, belief, and evidence, how faith figures into the study of knowledge and understanding, and detailed consideration of these topics as exemplified in real-world problems.

title: Ethical Issues of the Modern World
description: This course provides an introduction to moral philosophy and its application to contemporary ethical issues. At various times the course may focus on special topics. The course may be taken twice, provided the topics are different.

title: Astronomy
description: This course is an introductory survey of astronomy, the foundations of which include motions of the planets, the Moon, the Sun, and the stars; historical development of astronomy; an overview of the solar system; analyzing starlight; description, formation, evolution, and fate of stars; and an overview of the galaxies. Other topics may include planetary geology, the formation of the solar system, the search for planets around other stars, the big bang, expansion of the universe, dark matter, dark energy, and life in the universe. The Newhard Planetarium will be used to simulate motions of celestial bodies. Observing sessions may supplement classroom activities.

title: Philosophy of Law: Jurisprudence
description: This course offers a critical introduction to philosophy of law. Students will explore the views of diverse and influential thinkers from Plato to the present on the nature of law, associated problems, and types of solutions that have been proposed. The primary objective will be to better understand the function of law in human society. Topics will include the source of the authority of law, different types of law, the concept of legal responsibility, and the relationship between morality and law, as well as related subjects such as legal reasoning, legal rights, and theories of punishment.

title: Independent Study in Philosophy
description: This course is a program of guided independent study in an area selected to meet student interests and needs. The course is repeatable up to 10 hours, provided the topics are different.

title: Capstone Course
description: Students are expected to narrow the focus of their studies to an area of interest that they have developed during their previous course work and/or through their experience in the field. Students will then complete a project related to this area of interest. Ordinarily, this project will be a philosophical paper worthy of publication, although it could be some other endeavor befitting a culminating university experience. All projects will be conceived and executed in consultation with and guidance by the course instructor and will require approval by the director of philosophy. This is a repeatable course.

title: General Physics I/Lecture
description: This course is a study of linear and rotational kinematics and dynamics including Newton's Laws of Motion, conservation of momentum, conservation of energy, heat, and fluids. Emphasis is placed on conceptual understanding, application of physical laws, and problem solving.

title: General Physics II/Lecture
description: This course is a continuation of PHYS 250 and is the study of oscillations, waves, sound, light, optics, electricity, and magnetism. Some modern physics topics may also be covered. Emphasis is placed on conceptual understanding, application of physical laws, and problem solving.

title: Conceptual Physics/Lab
description: This course provides students taking PHYS 150 , Conceptual Physics, with hands-on experience with topics covered in the lectures and trains students in basic lab skills.

title: Conceptual Physics
description: This course is a conceptual study of basic physics principles with a foundation in mechanics, including Newton's Laws of Motion, momentum, energy, and rotation. Other topics will be selected and may include fluids, heat transfer, electricity, magnetism, and light. Conceptual applications of physics in everyday experience and in students' subject areas will be emphasized.

title: General Physics II/Lab
description: A variety of lab experiences are coordinated with topics covered in lectures and problems. In these labs data will be gathered, organized, and analyzed.

title: Calculus in Physics I
description: This course introduces calculus-based techniques to topics from PHYS 250 such as kinematics, dynamics, work, energy, fluids, and thermodynamics. Completion of PHYS 250 and this course provides a calculus-based physics experience equivalent to that of PHYS 252 .

title: Calculus-Based Physics I
description: This is a calculus-based physics course covering kinematics, dynamics, gravitation, rotating systems, momentum, work and energy, and fluids. Emphasis is placed on conceptual understanding, application of physical laws, and problem solving.  This class includes a laboratory component.

title: Physics for the Health Sciences
description: This course is a one-semester overview of vector mechanics, linear and rotational kinematics and dynamics, work, energy and power, wave phenomena, fluid mechanics, heat, electricity, and magnetism. Emphasis is placed upon the mathematical formulation of principles and on problem solving as well as their relationship to biological processes.

title: Calculus-Based Physics II
description: This course is a continuation of PHYS 252 . Topics covered will be the same as those in PHYS 251 , except calculus and vector algebra will be used as tools in lectures and problem solving.

title: Calculus in Physics II
description: This course introduces calculus-based techniques to topics from PHYS 251 such as wave phenomena, electricity, and magnetism. Completion of PHYS 251 and this course provides a calculus-based physics experience equivalent to that of PHYS 253 .

title: Advanced Topics
description: This course is a study of selected areas of physics not included in regular offerings. The course may be taken twice, provided the topics are different.

title: Introduction to Law
description: This course will introduce students to the American legal system and its relation to American politics, culture, and society. The course is to serve as an introduction to the concept of law, the philosophy of law, legal precedent, common law, the interpretation of law, the U.S. Supreme Court, and the practical functioning of the U.S. legal system. The course will also provide practical advice on U.S. law schools and the admissions process. Students will be introduced to the basic skills necessary for the understanding and practice of the law, including analytical interpretation, reading comprehension, rhetorical skills, legal and technical writing, and logical reasoning.

title: Biophysics
description: This course explores phenomena at the intersection of physics, biology, and chemistry. Students will enhance and develop their understanding of biological functions within the physical contexts of forces, energy, temperature, thermodynamics, viscosity, diffusion, pressure, and charge. Specific topics in biophysics will be explored and may include molecular motors, macromolecular structures and folding, ion pumps, action potentials and nerve impulses, and other topics of biophysical relevance.

title: International and Comparative Law
description: This course will focus on public and comparative international law. Students will examine the law of nations (for example the law of treaties, the law of war, human rights law, international environmental law) as well as compare and contrast leading representatives of the world's legal traditions (common law, civil law, non-western legal regimes).

title: Law and Public Service
description: This course introduces the legal and ethical requirements of practicing law in the United States. It also introduces public service requirements for ethics, transparency, fairness, and the public’s right to participate.

title: Public Policy Analysis
description: Federal and state public agencies must use detailed analysis to evaluate the cost of their regulations on the public, to evaluate alternatives, to seek public comment, and to make a final policy decision. Additional regulations may require an environmental or health and safety review. This course will introduce students to these requirements and teach basic analytic skills for determining need, alternative solutions, and their impacts.

title: Community-Urban Field Experiences in Law
description: This course offers majors in law and the liberal arts curriculum guided work-study experiences in the legal field. While most opportunities are available in Findlay and Hancock County, many off-campus learning experiences may also be available nationwide. This course will be graded S/U and may be repeated with a maximum of 15 semester hours applying toward a degree.

title: General Physics I/Lab
description: A variety of lab experiences are coordinated with topics covered in lectures and problems. In these labs data will be gathered, organized, and analyzed.

title: Constitutional Law and Judicial Advocacy: Moot Court
description: This course is designed to introduce substantive and jurisdictional concepts of the United States Supreme Court, and techniques of appellate oral argument. Students will analyze a simulated court case before the United States Supreme Court, plus other set Supreme Court cases, and develop arguments for the petitioner and the respondent in the simulated case. Students will participate in a local, regional, or national moot court competition. This course is repeatable.

title: Topics in Pre-Law
description: Topics pertaining to the study of law which may include comparative law, legal ethics, law in popular culture, law and literature, or some aspect of substantive law (e.g., property law). This course may be repeated, if the topic is different.

title: Legal Research and Writing
description: This course examines the basic legal resources relevant to law (such as Supreme Court reporters) and requires students to use such resources in writing legal briefs and research papers. Emphasis will be placed on legal briefs, appellate briefs, and law review style articles.

title: Introduction to Political Science
description: This course will introduce students to the discipline of political science. The various subfields and related fields will be reviewed: political theory, methodology, American politics, comparative politics, international relations, and public administration. Also, students will gain perspective on the historical development of this field of study, be introduced to various research methodologies, and learn the basics of accepted writing in politics and political science.

title: Jurisprudence: Philosophy of Law
description: This course is designed to introduce diverse and influential thinkers from Plato to present on the nature of law, associated problems, and legal solutions. The primary objective of the class is to better understand the function of law in human society. Individual topics include the source of the authority of law, different types of law, the concept of legal responsibility, and the relationship between morality and law, as well as related subjects such as legal reasoning, legal rights, and theories of punishment.

title: American Politics
description: This course provides analysis of the context of national politics in the United States and the institutions of American national government.

title: Independent Research in Law
description: This course allows students to pursue a specialized research interest with law and liberal arts faculty. Students will identify a viable research interest in some aspect of law. Students will be expected to follow up on this interest with a research proposal to investigate a meaningful question related to the law. Finally, students will demonstrate their research findings in a paper or other significant work product at the end of the semester. This course may be repeated if the topic is different and with the consent of the law faculty.

title: International Relations
description: This course offers an introduction to the analysis of international relations with case studies of current problems in world politics.

title: Comparative Politics
description: This course is an introduction to comparative political analysis with special attention devoted to politics in Western Europe, Russia, China, and selected developing states.

title: Judicial Process
description: This course offers a survey of the nature, function, and characteristic procedures of Anglo-American course of law. Development and types of law, jurisprudence, judicial selection, structure, and decision-making processes of the courts are discussed. The dynamics and operation of the American court system are analyzed within a historical and contemporary context.

title: Political Parties and Pressure Groups
description: This course involves a study of the history, organization, and functions of political parties and pressure groups. It provides an examination of the methods of political action with emphasis on nomination, election, campaign finance, and interest articulation.

title: Contemporary Political Ideologies
description: This course provides an analysis of political belief systems such as fascism, communism, socialism, liberalism, conservatism, and anarchism.

title: Internship in Political Science
description: This course involves guided work-study experiences. While most opportunities are available in Findlay and Hancock County, many off-campus learning experiences are also provided throughout Ohio and other parts of the United States. Internships through The Washington Center are also appropriate for political science internship credit. This course will be graded S/U and may be repeated with a maximum of 15 semester hours applying toward a degree.

title: Political Philosophy
description: This course explores the fundamental questions of politics through a close reading of classic philosophic texts. Are human beings by nature political animals or is the political community an artificial construct designed to help us escape from nature?  What is the relationship of wisdom to consent?  What is the source for what we call natural rights?  These are some of the questions that will be addressed in a course that will ultimately seek to understand the development of the modern state.

title: American Constitutional Law
description: This course is a study of Supreme Court cases relating to American governmental structure and process.

title: Topics: American Political Issues and Institutions
description: This course will allow students to explore in more detail topics and political institutions introduced in the American Politics course, for example: Congress, the presidency, public opinion, political socialization, political culture, or women in American politics. This is a repeatable course, as long as the topics are different. The course may not be taken more than twice.

title: Topics: Contemporary International Political Issues
description: This course involves study of relevant and current issues in the world arena, such as foreign policy, disarmament, alliances, energy, environment, and international law. Emphasis on one significant topic each time the course is offered. The course may be repeated, if topic is different.

title: The Politics of Terrorism
description: Low-intensity conflict is a significant characteristic of the contemporary international system. In the post-World War II period, scholars have accorded renewed attention to the study of terrorism as one form of this type of conflict. This course will provide an overview of the academic literature that addresses the following aspects of political terrorism:  history, causes, theories, strategies and tactics, methods of prevention, and the future of the phenomenon.

title: Seminar in Political Science
description: The course is designed and required as a capstone experience for all criminal justice, law, and the liberal arts, and political science majors. Students are asked to narrow their focus to a significant area of research in their discipline relating to interests developed during previous course work, internships, etc. After determination of this area of interest, students are then tasked with the completion of a project relating to this narrowed topic. The project can be a research paper (utilizing either qualitative or quantitative methodology) or some other creative and useful endeavor agreed upon by the course instructor and student (e.g., development and implementation of high school workshops, development of course lesson plans, case study creation, etc.). This is a repeatable course.

title: General Psychology
description: This course offers an orientation to psychology as the scientific study of behavior and mental processes, with special emphasis on principles of learning, memory, adjustment, abnormal behavior, and the physiology of behavior.

title: Area Studies
description: This course offers an examination of contemporary political, social, and economic issues and problems in world regions, such as the Middle East, Latin America, Africa, and Western Europe. The course may be repeated, if topic is different.

title: Orientation to Psychology
description: Designed for psychology majors, or those thinking of psychology as a major, to accompany PSYC 100 , this course will offer the starting point for the building of the student's portfolio and assessment process and will provide an overview of the major as well as the various career opportunities available.

title: State and Local Politics
description: This course offers analysis of state, metropolitan, and community politics with special emphasis on problems of public policy in education, transportation, urban development, and social welfare.

title: Abnormal Psychology
description: This course covers the study of pathological behavior generally, with major emphasis on the nature, causes, prevention, and treatment of mental disorders.

title: Psy-Key Club
description: The Psy-Key Club will provide opportunities for psychology majors, minors, and others interested in psychology to learn more about psychology and to participate in activities that promote skill building in working with people. This course is repeatable and graded S/U.

title: Lifespan Development
description: This course traces the physiological, cognitive, and psychosocial development of the individual from conception through late adulthood. Choices and obstacles relative to normal growth and development are examined.

title: Social Psychology
description: This course covers the study of human behavior in a social setting. Interpersonal relations are considered in the light of principles of attitude formation, group dynamics, leadership, etc.

title: Animal Behavior
description: This course will provide the student with a general background of the field of animal behavior, including theoretical perspectives and applied examples. Study will cross the taxa, with special attention to domesticated species.

title: Readings in Psychology
description: This course has no specific content area; rather its content is chosen by the student via his or her special interest. It may involve an individual or group approach. This is a repeatable course and is graded S/U.

title: Research Methods I: Design and Analysis
description: This course is an introductory analysis of methods, techniques, and procedures used in behavioral science research. The material covers the basics of hypothesis testing and research design as well as the descriptive and inferential statistical procedures most relevant to research in psychology. Data entry and analysis using SPSS are also covered.

title: Health Psychology
description: This course focuses on the psychological factors that impact the health of children and adults. Students learn how psychological research methods, theories, and principles can be applied to enhance biomedical approaches for promoting health and treating illness. Students become aware of the experiences of treating patients in the hospital setting, factors that affect adherence to medical regimens, and sources of problems in patient/practitioner relationships. Individual behavioral change projects and group research are assigned to acquaint students with the methods needed to create healthier lifestyles.

title: Clinical Psychology
description: This course is an introduction to how psychological theory, methods, and practice are applied in clinical or counseling sessions. The major focus will be on approaches for applying psychology to mental health issues, psychological procedures for assessment and treatment, and how to build rapport and develop interpersonal relationships. This course would be useful for students entering the helping fields.

title: Cognitive Psychology
description: This course examines the key issues associated with the scientific study of cognitive psychology including research methodology, neurology, perception, attention, memory, language, and problem solving.

title: Theories of Personality
description: This course offers an integrated approach to the structure, dynamics, and development of personality as viewed by the major theorists of the day. Research methodology is considered, as well as major determinants of personality development.

title: Psychology Internship
description: This course involves guided work-study field or service-learning experiences outside the normal classroom setting. Up to 15 hours may be earned in one semester. Opportunities exist in the Findlay area or throughout the United States. This course will be graded S/U and may be repeated with a maximum of 15 semester hours applying toward a degree. Students will meet periodically as a group with the internship director.

title: Applied Sport Psychology
description: This course will introduce students to the field of applied sport psychology from the perspective of a coach. Application of sport psychology theories will be applied to the areas of leadership, motivation, decision making, group effectiveness and team-building, peak performance, arousal, imagery, and concentration. Prospective coaches, physical or occupational therapists, athletic trainers, and student-athletes could benefit from this course.

title: History of Psychology
description: This course covers the history of human behavior from early interpretations by the Greeks and others to modern scientific psychology. The emergence of psychology as an independent discipline apart from the Natural Philosophy is highlighted.

title: Sensation and Perception
description: This course focuses on the processes of sensation and perception and the laboratory studies associated with these processes. Sensation can be defined as the processes involved in converting physical energy into neural signals. Perception can be defined as the processes involved in creating internal experiences that correlate with the physical world around us.

title: Brain and Behavior
description: The course is meant to survey critical concepts regarding the relationship between brain functions and behavior. The topics covered over the course of the semester will include basic physiological functions of the nervous system, an overview of brain anatomy involved with sensation and perception, higher-level cognition, as well as emotion and neurological/psychological disorders.

title: Child and Adolescent Development
description: This is an advanced course in the field of developmental psychology. Observational, survey, or interview research is utilized for experiential learning. Special attention will be given to how the science of development applies in context.

title: Evolutionary Psychology
description: The course will examine the major tenets of Evolutionary Psychology and how the evolutionary perspective influences our perceptions of self. Of particular note will be the evaluation of the hypothesis that there is a mismatch between the evolved human brain and the pressures of modern society.

title: Research Methods II: Experimentation
description: The course is intended to advance the student's understanding of experimental research methods. The course covers basic and advanced principles of experimental design. The research process is highlighted from forming initial conceptions to publishing professional manuscripts.

title: Senior Capstone Seminar
description: This capstone course offers students the opportunity to evaluate where they have been, where they are now, and where they want to be personally and professionally. Students will also select a semester-long project, research study, or extensive field experience that utilizes their skills and integrates their learning in psychology.

title: Studies in Behavior
description: This course covers topics from the mainstream of contemporary experimental psychology. Each specific topic is subtitled on the student's transcript. Examples of the subtitles are drugs, psychology of religious behavior, and psychology and the law. The course may be repeated, provided the topic is different.

title: Seminar
description: This is an opportunity for the advanced student to pursue independent research and to do a special project or to be involved in a specific group topic or workshop. This is a repeatable course and is graded S/U.

title: Advanced Topics in Psychology
description: This course covers advanced topics from contemporary psychology. Each specific topic is subtitled on the student's transcript. Examples of the subtitles are cybernetic minds, rational choice in unstable environments, and human/animal consciousness. The course may be repeated up to eight hours, provided the topic is different.

title: Psychological Research
description: The student will develop, with the aid of a research mentor selected from one of the psychology faculty, a question to be researched. The student will then gather the necessary data to answer the question, and present the results orally and in writing to the psychology faculty and peers. This is a repeatable course.

title: Quest for Meaning
description: This course grapples with a vital question at the heart of the arts and humanities: What is the meaning of life? This course guides the quest for meaning from the insights of various religious works and figures. Throughout this course, students will be equipped with a variety of perspectives that may serve them well in their own quests for meaning.

title: Hindu and Buddhist Traditions
description: This is an introductory survey of the history, philosophies, cultures, and rituals of Hindu and Buddhist traditions. Students will also learn how these traditions continue to influence and function in the contemporary world.

title: Introduction to Religion
description: This course introduces students to various religions of the world, including Hinduism, Judaism, Buddhism, Daoism (Taoism), Christianity, and Islam: their basic beliefs, texts, practices, common concerns, and relevance for contemporary students. While studying these religious traditions, students will explore the enduring questions of life, the search for meaning and identity, questions of evil and suffering, and what it means to seek the common good.

title: The Jewish and Christian Traditions
description: This course is an introduction to the Jewish and Christian traditions, their history, central teachings and practices, as well as their distinctive and shared values. The course also examines Jewish-Christian dialogue in the post-Holocaust world. While studying these religious traditions, students will explore the enduring questions of life, the search for meaning and identity, questions of evil and suffering, and what it means to seek the common good.

title: Regional and Ancestral Religious Traditions
description: This course is a survey of non-dominant religious traditions including African, Native American, Caribbean, Japanese, and Australian. Some of the traditions studied include Zulu, Yoruba, Vodou (voodoo), Aboriginal, and Shinto traditions.

title: Interpreting the Bible
description: This course provides a critical introduction to various methods, multidisciplinary approaches, theoretical models, and research tools available for informed readings of the Bible.

title: Religion and Pop Culture: Methods and Theories in the Study of Religion
description: This course introduces students to various methods and theories in the study of religion and directly applies these methods and theories to depictions of religion(s) that originate in popular culture. Throughout this course, students will study the history of the word “religion” and how this field of study developed in multidisciplinary ways. Students will gain new understandings of ways in which humans have sought meaning and purpose historically, continuing to the present day—while primarily being guided by the questions, “What is religion?” and “What qualities and characteristics qualify someone/something as religious?” Each semester’s course will present Religious Studies methodologies through a particular pop culture lens.

title: Christian Theology
description: This course offers a study of the central teaching of the Christian faith, with attention to their classical formulations as well as more recent interpretations.

title: History of Christianity
description: This course involves a study of Orthodoxy, Roman Catholicism, and Protestant traditions of Christianity.

title: The Islamic Tradition
description: This course is an introductory survey of the history and variety of Islamic traditions, from the lifetime of the Prophet in seventh-century Arabia to contemporary Islamic communities in the Middle East, South Asia, and North America.

title: The New Testament
description: This course offers an introduction to the historical development, religion, and literature of the New Testament. The New Testament is studied in English translation, with special attention to its first century Jewish and Greco-Roman context as well as its major teachings and central concerns. In particular, students explore the nature of faith and the earliest Christian call to service and generosity, especially on behalf of the poor.

title: Special Topics in Religious Studies
description: Each time this course is offered, it will focus on a specific topic in religious studies. The course may be taken more than once, provided the topics are different.

title: The Hebrew Bible (Old Testament)
description: This course provides a critical study of the Hebrew Bible (Old Testament) and the religion of ancient Israel within the context of other ancient Near Eastern traditions. The Hebrew Bible is read in English translation with special attention to historical, literary, and ethical issues. In particular, students explore grand narratives of meaning, divine promise, faith and the common good, expressions of worship and wisdom, the problem of evil, and the prophetic call to compassion, social justice, and love for neighbor.

title: The Buddhist Tradition
description: This course is an intermediate survey of the history and variety of Buddhist traditions, from the lifetime of the Buddha in fifth-century BCE India to contemporary Buddhist communities in East Asia, Southeast Asia, and North America.

title: Prophets of Ancient Israel
description: This course offers an intensive study of the writing of the prophets in the Hebrew Bible (Old Testament) with special attention given to their historical, cultural, and religious settings. The course also examines ways in which the words of the prophets transcend their own context to address individuals and communities today.

title: Religious Issues in Contemporary Society
description: This topically-structured course will provide the student with the opportunity to reflect on the contemporary influence of religious values and the integral role it has in various global, social, economic, and political systems. This is a repeatable course.

title: Pentateuch
description: This course studies the Pentateuch and the early historical books of the Old Testament. It pays special attention to the theme of the covenant relationship between God and Israel.

title: Wisdom and Poetry in the Hebrew Bible (in English)
description: The course is a critical study of the Wisdom literature (Job, Ecclesiastes, Proverbs) and the Psalter of the Hebrew Bible (Old Testament) with emphasis on questions of ultimate meaning, values, human suffering, and the spirituality of ancient Israel.

title: New Religious Movements
description: This course is an intermediate, cross-cultural survey of new religious movements that have emerged, or are presently emerging, from established religious traditions.

title: Daoist and Confucian Traditions
description: This is an introductory survey of the history, philosophies, cultures, and rituals of Daoist and Confucian traditions. Students will also learn how these traditions continue to influence and function in our contemporary world.

title: Jesus: A Historical, Literary, and Cultural Survey
description: This course undertakes a historical, critical, and literary survey of the Gospels of Matthew, Mark, Luke, and John in order to discern early understandings of Jesus and how he is uniquely portrayed in each gospel. This course also seeks to identify and understand the diverse global cultural portrayals and understandings of Jesus throughout history.

title: Gospel of John and General Epistles
description: Students read the Johannine literature - the Gospel and Epistles of John and Revelation. They also examine the general epistles of Hebrews, James, Peter, and Jude.

title: Travel Seminar in Religious Studies
description: This is a travel course organized by religious studies faculty in which various important sites of religious cultures are visited, studied, and researched. The course may be taken three times provided that the travel sites are different. (Three hours may be used for GE credit.)

title: Acts and Pauline Literature
description: Students survey the origin and history of the early church as recorded in Acts. They also study Paul's epistles in relationship to the issues in the church that the apostle addressed.

title: Leadership Formation: The Ethic of Compassion
description: This course examines the ethic of compassion as essential to effective leadership through the lens of various religious traditions. Students will examine which religious ethics guided effective leaders, such as Siddhartha Gautama, Jesus, Mahatma Gandhi, Dr. Martin Luther King, Jr., the Dalai Lama, and Archbishop Desmond Tutu. Students will examine the ethic of compassion in its religious and philosophical history and how it became central to so many models of religious leadership.

title: The Bible as Literature
description: This course is a literary study of the Bible (both Testaments), with emphasis on rhetoric, characters, plot, setting, metaphors, and themes. The focus of the course is on close readings of selected narratives and poems in the Bible. Biblical texts are examined in their final literary form, with less emphasis on reconstructing political, social, and religious ideas that lie beneath the surface of the text.

title: Liturgical Engagement
description: This course offers an examination of homiletic models based on recent exegetical, hermeneutical, and intercommunication theories.

title: Holistic Care and Counseling
description: This course introduces students to viable expressions of caring ministry based on biblical, theological, and recent theoretical models.

title: Field Experiences in Religion
description: This is an active, experience-oriented service-learning course in which students extend their study of religion through a field work project approved and supervised by religious studies faculty. The project also must be supervised on site by a representative of the sponsoring agency. The course is offered in units of one semester hour; may be taken for a total of five hours.

title: Christian Ethics
description: This course is designed to identify and evaluate methods of moral decision-making and Scriptural models for moral deliberation and will include case studies of specific moral issues.

title: Revenue Streams and Sales of Sport Business Strategy
description: This course is designed to introduce and expose students to the key elements of designing, planning, marketing, and complete operations of tickets sales, box office management, and event sales.

title: Research in Religious Studies
description: This course provides a critical study and research in religious studies, either independent or directed. The course may be taken twice, provided the topics are different.

title: Principles of Sport Business Strategy
description: This course provides a foundation in the principles and significance of marketing and revenue generation in the sports through a mix of theoretical fundamentals and practical application. It covers key marketing elements as well as revenue generation in sports through sales process, relationship-building and customer service, and business development.

title: Brand Management
description: This course provides an overview of core elements of marketing along with essential components of the benefits of promotion and sales, sponsorship contact, relation, and sales. Licensing is also discussed along with managing the sales of sport brands/images. Special events are discussed relative to the activity being conducted.

title: Business of College Athletics
description: This course will help students understand college athletics at all levels (i.e., NCAA Division I, II, III, and NAIA), the role of athletics at the university, and the rules and regulations universities must follow.

title: Religious Studies Capstone Project
description: Students in this course with the guidance of the course instructor will conduct research on a topic related to coursework done in their junior or senior year. Each student will then complete a project related to their individual area of interest culminating in a substantial research paper, a creative project, or some other endeavor befitting a culminating University experience.

title: Sports Analytics and Data Visualization
description: In this course, students will learn about careers in sports analytics and how analytics is used in the sports business industry via interactions with a variety of professionals within the industry. Students will investigate sport operations and athletic performance using multiple data analytic approaches. During this course, students will explore the skills, technologies, applications, and practices essential to understand and evaluate performance in sport organizations as well as athletic performance to formulate suitable models, develop and interpret solutions, and communicate results.

title: Business of Baseball
description: This course provides students the opportunity to explore the managerial aspects of operating a baseball team, facility, and events. This course combines classroom instruction with hands-on service learning on how to operate a summer collegiate baseball team.

title: Principles of Personal Training
description: This course is designed to establish a foundation of the principles and guidelines for personal training. The aim is to apply the principles of functional anatomy and basic exercise science to the general populations, wherein the students will learn and practice the skills for safe client assessment, program design, motivation, education, and legal parameters of the personal training industry. The emphasis is on one-on-one trainer/client interaction; the skills of which can be applied to a variety of exercise settings.

title: Practicum: Personal Training
description: This course will be at an on-site location in which an individual will directly work with individuals with an exercise program. One hundred hours of field experience is required in this course.

title: Christian Worship
description: This course introduces students to expressions of worship in Catholic, Protestant, and Evangelical contexts. Students develop a "theology of worship" that is contextually appropriate to their own faith tradition.

title: Introduction to Exercise Technique
description: This course uses a systematic approach to teaching resistance/strength training movements. The practical application of anatomy, physiology, and body alignment is introduced to emphasize correct performance of various exercise and spotting techniques, analyze strength training techniques and various pieces of exercise equipment for appropriate incorporation into a comprehensive training plan, and to conduct a risk/benefit analysis for specific techniques and exercises.

title: Practicum: Athletic Facility Management
description: This course will be at an on-site location in which the individual will learn about sales, daily maintenance, and management of a facility. One hundred hours of field experience is required for this course.

title: Exercise Prescription for Special Populations
description: This course explores the pathophysiology of a variety of clinical and specialized populations.  The response to exercise and standardized exercise prescriptions will be examined, as well as the role of the exercise professional in the continuum of care.

title: Foundations in Strength and Conditioning
description: This course establishes a scientific foundation for the principles of training and exercise science as they apply to strength and conditioning.  Utilizing this foundation, students will learn to apply critical thinking to achieve specific training adaptations to improve sport performance.

title: Practicum: Strength and Conditioning
description: This course is designed for the student to put principles of cardiovascular training and strength training into practice as they relate to sport team training. Students will work with a university sport team for which they will design a fitness program based on individual and team goals. Students will be tested on their own weight-lifting techniques. Students will complete a final project designing a strength and conditioning program for an athletic team.

title: Contemporary Social Problems
description: This course provides an analysis of current issues such as family disorganization, poverty, suicide, prostitution, alcoholism, drug addiction, and aging.

title: Strength and Conditioning II
description: This course explores advanced programming and periodization concepts with an emphasis on elite athletes. An in-depth examination of the process of periodization and the variables of program design as they pertain to a wide-variety of sports will be considered.

title: Strength and Conditioning I
description: This course examines the sports science behind and stemming from the foundations of strength and conditioning.   Advanced considerations for exercise programming and periodization will be introduced and explored through the lens of exercise science as they relate both related to the individual athlete and the team environment.

title: Topics in Sociology
description: This course is designed to enable students and faculty in the department to explore selected topics in sociology. Students from outside the discipline are also invited. The content of the course will vary and will be determined by timely issues and developments within the field and by the special interests of students and faculty. This course presumes no previous background in sociology. This is a repeatable course.

title: Senior Seminar in Strength and Conditioning
description: This course ties program instructional and practicum components together within the program curriculum and provides an enhanced synergy to separate course components. This course will focus on practical aspects of the strength and conditioning and human performance professions.

title: Social Stratification
description: This course provides an intensive analysis of the patterns by which property, power, and prestige are allocated in society. It includes the meaning of stratification for society as a whole and for groups within society. Discussion of social mobility, class consciousness, and other selected topics.

title: Juvenile Delinquency
description: This course offers a sociological analysis of delinquency in the United States with specific attention to behavioral patterns and their societal roots; the juvenile justice system; juvenile law (including Ohio law); and the institutional means of control, treatment, and prevention.

title: Culture in America
description: This course will focus on such groups as African-Americans, Native Americans, Hispanics, and women. It includes anthropological and sociological views of race and cultural differences and patterns of ethnic relationships.

title: Community-Urban Field Experiences
description: This course involves guided work-study experiences in urban areas. While most opportunities are available in Findlay and Hancock County, many off-campus learning experiences are also provided throughout Ohio and other parts of the United States. This course will be graded S/U and may be repeated with a maximum of 15 semester hours applying toward a degree.

title: Sociology of the Family
description: This course covers an analysis of the American family as an ongoing institution subject to external pressures for change. Intra-family dynamics also will be considered. Other topics include theories and frames of reference in family sociology, mate selection, the family life cycle, sexual relationships, socialization of children, divorce, alternate family forms, and the family of the future.

title: Urban Sociology
description: This course involves sociological study of the emergence and development of urban centers and suburbs. Emphasis is placed on the social problems of the inner city, social stratification and social mobility, poverty, overcrowding and slums, transportation, congestion, and environmental pollution. Analysis of alternative solutions to these problems, planning, urban renewal, and housing development are covered in this course.

title: Topics in Sociology
description: This course is designed to enable students and faculty in the department to explore selected topics in sociology. Students from outside the discipline are also invited. The content of the course will vary and will be determined by timely issues and developments within the field and by the special interests of students and faculty. This course may be repeated if topic is different.

title: Society and Sexuality
description: This course examines the ways in which human sexuality is influenced by cultural/social factors and how people react to and create the meanings which are a part of our sexual nature. Specific topics may include sex education, sexual violence, prostitution, pornography, birth control, and AIDS.

title: Criminal Behavior
description: This course provides an analysis of the causes, nature, and effects of criminal behavior. An in-depth examination of the major theoretical perspectives is provided.

title: Readings in Sociology
description: The content and structure of this course is not predetermined. An agreement between the appropriate faculty member and the student will establish the direction, depth, and expectations of the course. The course may be repeated for credit as long as the content area of the reading has changed. Graded S/U.

title: Introduction to Social Work
description: This course is an overview of social work emphasizing the commonalities of a very diverse profession. It focuses on the historical development of the profession, the variety of practice settings and levels of practice, and the knowledge, ethics, values, and skills required for entry-level professional practice.

title: Supervised Research
description: Designed primarily for individualized research, this course will ordinarily involve the preparation of an empirical research project. It will include the construction of a research proposal, the collection of data to test hypotheses, and the presentation of a final written report. This course may, on occasion, be designed to enable the student to report on library-oriented research.

title: Social Work Club
description: Promotion and encouragement of discussion and action on contemporary social issues. Participation in selected volunteer experiences in the community. This course is graded S/U. This is a repeatable course.

title: The Origin and Development of Social Theory
description: This course covers the emergence and growth of social science thought and its application to contemporary issues. Special emphasis is given to the writing of early social scientists and their contemporary counterparts. The purpose of this course is to encourage advanced social science students, and others who may be interested, to synthesize their own ideas in relation to the thinking of these major figures.

title: Introduction to Sociology
description: This course is recommended as a first course for those not familiar with the field of sociology. Emphasis is on basic concepts and processes in sociology. Topics include social organization and the relation of the individual to society, socialization, roles, group processes, stratification, social authority and control, disorganization, and change.

title: Social Work Practice I
description: This course will cover the principles and skills of generalist social work practice with individuals and families. The student will learn the problem-solving process and will develop skills in engagement, assessment, planning, implementation, evaluation, and termination.

title: Social Work Interviewing Skills
description: Complementing SOWK 305 , this course will allow the student to review and implement the techniques of the social work interview with a particular focus upon individuals and families. This course is taught laboratory style, with role-play, videotaping, and class exercises. This is a repeatable course.

title: Topics in Social Work
description: This course is designed to enable students and faculty to explore interdisciplinary topics relevant to social work. Topics will be alternated and may include, but are not limited to, such issues as child welfare, substance abuse, family violence, gay and lesbian issues, the law, and health care. This is a repeatable course.

title: Social Work Practice II
description: Entry-level social workers are expected to work within a wide variety of situations involving groups. This course will help develop students into group leaders having the requisite knowledge of group behavior, structure, and dynamics, and the leadership skills necessary to conduct both task and treatment groups.

title: American Social Service Policy
description: Viewed from program and philosophical bases, this course focuses on the contemporary social welfare systems in the United States at all levels of planning and delivery. Special emphasis is on methods of evaluating service delivery. Attention is also given to social policies of other countries and how they compare to those of the United States.

title: Field Placement I
description: The student will complete six hours per week (at least 60 hours during the semester) of observation within a social service agency, supervised by a professional social worker. The student will also spend three hours per week in a classroom setting in addition to the time spent in the agency. This course will be graded S/U. This is a repeatable course.

title: Meeting the Needs of Children
description: This course offers an examination of the concepts, policies, and practices designed to meet the diverse needs of children and families through a network of programs and social services, supplementary services, and substitute services. Issues for future planning are also considered, including preservation of the home culture. Content of the course is particularly relevant for all who will work with children (e.g., social workers, teachers, nurses, clergy, etc.).

title: Readings in Social Work
description: The content and structure of this course are not predetermined. A formal agreement between the instructor and the student prior to enrollment will establish direction, depth, and requirements of the course. This course may be repeated, if the content of the reading has changed.

title: Human Behavior in the Social Environment I
description: The course involves the analysis of human behavior and the social context within which the behavior of individuals and families occurs throughout the lifespan. Developmental characteristics as well as theoretical perspectives from which to view behavior are examined. Specific approaches to be covered include biological, psychological, sociological, ecological-systems theory, and family-systems theory.

title: Topics in Social Work
description: This course is designed to enable students and faculty to explore selected topics relevant to social work practice. Topics may include, but are not limited to, substance abuse, family violence, gay and lesbian concerns, geriatrics, the law, and health care. This course may be repeated, if topic is different. Travel/study courses are also offered.

title: Human Behavior in the Social Environment II
description: This course is the second of a two-course sequence focusing on the person-in-environment. This half of the sequence will analyze human behavior in the social context within which it occurs outside of the family, that is, in larger groups, organizations, and communities. It will also explore the ways in which individuals are affected by the social systems that surround them, with a particular emphasis on the impact of oppression on at-risk populations.

title: Spanish Club
description: The Spanish Club is an organization of The University of Findlay students that promotes awareness of the language and culture of the Hispanic people. Spanish Club members actively participate in University activities such as High School Spanish Day, International Day, International Night, and Hispanic Heritage Week. This course is graded S/U. This is a repeatable course.

title: Social Work Practice III
description: This course is the third in a sequence of courses dealing with professional social work practice. In this course, the student learns to apply the generalist methods of practice at the macro level, that is, with organizations, neighborhoods, and communities. Emphasis is also given to the impact of those systems on individuals, families, and groups.

title: Senior Integrative Seminar
description: This is the capstone course for social work majors, taken the second semester of the senior year. Its purpose is to enable the student to integrate social work knowledge, skills, and values and a liberal arts base into a set of practice competencies necessary for the successful performance of entry-level social work practice.

title: Social Work Field Instruction II
description: This course consists of the student's final field placement experience in a program-approved social service agency under the supervision of a professional social worker. As a learner, the student will gain and implement the skills, knowledge, values acquired in the curriculum, leading to professional, entry-level practice as a social worker. Students will spend at least 480 hours in the agency and will attend a two-hour-per-week seminar which will integrate previous course work and the practicum experience. This course will be graded S/U.

title: Spanish I
description: This introductory Spanish course is designed for non-native Spanish speakers and/or beginners with little to no previous knowledge of Spanish. The course focuses on developing the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages (ACTFL) proficiency benchmark of Novice Mid-High in Spanish reading, writing, speaking, and listening, with an emphasis on fostering an appreciation for Spanish-speaking cultures.

title: Senior Research Project
description: Taken during the final semester of the senior year, this course involves scientific research, including the formulation and completion of a scientific research project integrating the student's practice and senior field placement responsibilities.

title: Spanish for Professionals
description: Designed for students with little or no background in Spanish, this course serves students who need to communicate with the Spanish speaker in the various professions. This course integrates thematically related vocabulary and grammar and it is geared to Spanish for specific purposes. It has three basic purposes: to study the Spanish language with an emphasis on basic patterns; to acquire vocabulary especially useful in practical situations and professions; and to make comparisons of cultural similarities and differences of Hispanic countries and to present and discuss relevant aspects of culturally appropriate language use. The course topics vary by semester. (Does not count toward the major, minor, or license).

title: Spanish Film
description: Taught in English, this course surveys notable films (in Spanish with English subtitles) from the 20th and 21st centuries. Students will learn to employ comparative and rhetorical analysis to examine topics such as human nature, contemporary themes, and aesthetic and artistic expression. This course is repeatable up to six hours, as long as the topics are different.

title: Service Learning in Spanish
description: This course provides students with opportunities to serve the community through service learning projects and to further develop their language skills and deepen their cultural knowledge outside of classrooms. Projects may include service at schools, agencies, or businesses. Service projects must be approved by a supervising faculty prior to their beginning. This course may be repeated for up to six semester hours provided the projects are different. (A total of three hours may be counted for Spanish major or minor.)

title: Spanish Composition
description: This course provides intensive work on various styles of Spanish composition for academic and non-academic uses. It explores differences between oral and written communication. The course includes group work and discussions as well as frequent individual compositions.

title: Spanish III
description: This Spanish course is a continuation of Spanish II and focuses on furthering the development of Spanish language for nonnative Spanish speakers. The course focuses on developing the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages (ACTFL) proficiency benchmark of Intermediate High in Spanish reading, writing, speaking, and listening, with an emphasis on fostering an appreciation for Spanish-speaking cultures.

title: Spanish Reading, Writing, and Grammar
description: This course develops Spanish reading, writing, and grammar proficiency through literary analysis, formal writing assignments, and the study of advanced grammar in order to prepare students for upper-level culture and literature courses which require the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages (ACTFL) proficiency benchmark of Intermediate High – Advanced Mid.

title: Research Methods for Social Workers
description: This course provides introductory analysis of methods, techniques, and procedures of research for social workers, and the elementary descriptive and inductive statistical procedures that allow social work majors systematically to evaluate programs, policies, organizations, and their practice.

title: Spanish II
description: This Spanish course is a continuation of Spanish I and focuses on furthering the development of Spanish language for nonnative Spanish speakers and/or beginners. The course focuses on developing the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages (ACTFL) proficiency benchmark of Intermediate Low-Mid in Spanish reading, writing, speaking, and listening, with an emphasis on fostering an appreciation for Spanish-speaking cultures.

title: Spanish IV
description: This Spanish course is a continuation of Spanish III and focuses on furthering the development of Spanish language for nonnative Spanish speakers. The course focuses on developing the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages (ACTFL) proficiency benchmark of Advanced Low in Spanish reading, writing, speaking, and listening, with an emphasis on fostering an appreciation for Spanish-speaking cultures.

title: Culture of Spain (in Spanish)
description: Taught in Spanish, this course integrates the political, economic, social, geographical, and cultural forces that shape Spain.

title: Spanish Conversation and Pronunciation
description: This course is designed to provide students with an opportunity to improve pronunciation and conversational fluency in Spanish through communicative activities to advance the interpersonal and presentational Spanish-speaking fluency at the American Council on the Teaching of Foreign Languages (ACTFL) proficiency benchmark of Intermediate High – Advanced Mid levels.

title: Spanish Culture (in English)
description: Taught in English, this course integrates the political, economic, social, geographical, and cultural forces that shape Spain. (This course does not count for the Spanish major or minor.)

title: Spanish Experiences
description: This course surveys the geography, cuisine, music, art, and daily practices of various Spanish-Speaking countries. Students will be exposed to experiential learning activities. This course may be taken twice, provided the topics are different.

title: Spanish for Spanish Speakers
description: This course is designed to address the unique needs of the student who has grown up in an English-speaking culture but within a Spanish-speaking community and/or home. Readings, lectures, and classroom activities aim at giving the students a greater knowledge and appreciation of their own cultural and linguistic heritage, helping them distinguish between standard and non-standard Spanish, adding more standard terms to students' vocabulary, improving spelling and use of written accent marks, and giving students a second grammar to use in a professional or formal context.

title: Spanish for the Professions
